The pumps are always electrically driven because they need to deliver a high pressure of 3000 psi.
The filters, the pressure relief valve(s), the by-pass valve(s). and the fire shut-off valve are safety features installed in a typical system.

questions.questionText: In a hydraulic brake system of a transport aircraft, an accumulator is pre-charged to 1200 psi. 707/934 When powering up a hydraulic system, the level in the reservoir will:
1087934 Hydraulic fluids most frequently used in the systems of modern large transport airplanes are:

options: warn of a hydraulic system overheat.
warn of an impending by-pass situation.
indicate that the filter is clogged and unfiltered oil is passing around the system.
indicate that the filter is due for maintenance

questions.questionText: An airplane is taxiing with armed autobrakes (RTO mode) but needs to brake hard at a low speed before reaching the runway. What happens to the autobrake system?
options: The RTO mode will remain armed.
The autobrake system kicks in and brakes.
The autobrake system remains armed and it is not possible to brake normally below 15 kts.
The autobrake system is disarmed automatically.

questions.questionText: Which types of hydraulic fluids are commonly used in civil aviation?
111/934 A modern hydraulic system typically includes "hydraulic fuses". Their function is:
options: to prevent total system loss in case of a leaking hydraulic line.
to allow by-passing of a hydraulic pump in case it is subject to excessive pressure, without further damage to the system.
to switch to the secondary system in case of a leak in the primary brake system.
to isolate a part of the system and protect it against accidental contamination.

questions.questionText: 0113/934 Axial piston pumps are often used in hydraulic systems due to:
options: the safety feature of the quill drive shearing due to pump seizure and the ease of repair by replacement of pump and shaft only.
their ability to produce high pressure combined with constant high flow rate.
their ability to produce high pressure when required but can be off loaded to reduce power consumption.
their low cost, simplicity and durability

questions.questionText: The 'inflow valve' in a pressurization system controls:
options: the rate at which air enters the cabin.
the rate at which air leaves the cabin.
the temperature of the incoming air.

questions.questionText: Q114/934 The type of hydraulic fluid most frequently used in the hydraulic systems of modern transport aeroplanes is:
options: Mixture of mineral oil and alcohol.

questions.questionText: 115/934 Large transport aircraft hydraulic systems usually operate with a system pressure of approximately:
116/934 In a typical hydraulic system, the overheat detectors are usually installed:
Q117/934 In hydraulic systems of large modern transport category aircraft the fluids used are:

options: High flash point, low volatility, low compressibility, low viscosity and low flammability.
Low flash point, high volatility, high compressibility, low viscosity and low flammability.
High flash point, high volatility, low compressibility, high viscosity and low flammability.
Low flash point, low volatility, high compressibility, low viscosity and high flammability.

questions.questionText: In a hydraulic system, 'cavitation' is usually caused by:
options: air or vapor bubbles forming in the fluid due to low pressure at the pump inlet.
using the wrong type of fluid.

questions.questionText: Q119/934 Internal leakage in a hydraulic system will cause:
120/934 The Ram Air Turbine (RAT) provides emergency hydraulic power for:
options: nose wheel steering after the aeroplane has landed.
flight controls in the event of loss of engine driven hydraulic power.
undercarriage selection and automatic brake system.

questions.questionText: Refer to figure 021-14. In the typical hydraulic system represented by the illustration, assuming hydraulic pressure throughout and no internal leakage:
options: the jack will move to the left due to pressure acting on differential areas.
a condition of hydraulic lock exists and no movement of the jack will take place.
since pressures are equal, the jack is free to move in response to external forces.
the jack will move to the right due to equal pressure acting on differential areas.

questions.questionText: 122/934 For an aeroplane hydraulic supply circuit, the correct statement is:
options: the security components comprise the filters, the pressure relief valves, the by-passes, and the fire shut-off valve.
the reservoir constitutes a reserve of hydraulic fluid maintained under pressure by a pneumatic back pressure (air or nitrogen) and destined to serve as a fluid or pressure reserve.
the regulation system deals only with emergency operation and is not applied to all hydraulic services but only those considered as essential.
the pumps are always electric due to the high pressures which they must deliver (140 to 210 kg/cm²).

questions.questionText: 1237934 Should a hydraulic pump seize during operation:
options: the inner barrel of the pump will rotate thus offloading and protecting the gearbox.
a red light will illuminate in the cockpit to warn the pilot of the failure.
the quill drive will shear to offload and protect the gearbox
the freewheel unit will disengage the pump from the gearbox.

questions.questionText: 124/934 In hydraulic system, a SHUTTLE VALVE:
options: allows two units to be operated by one pressure source.
enables an alternate system to operate the same actuators in case of normal system failure.

questions.questionText: What does a 'brake temperature indicator' help the pilot avoid?
options: Brake fade or fire after heavy use.

questions.questionText: An accumulator in a hydraulic system will:
options: increase pressure surges within the system.
store fluid under pressure and allows for thermal expansion.

questions.questionText: 1267934 Hydraulic fluids:
options: are irritating to eyes and skin.
can be used in the lubrication system.

questions.questionText: The accumulator in a hydraulic system works as:
Hydraulic fluids used in aircraft are mainly synthetic and mineral fluids. Can both types of fluids be used together?
options: No, the system needs to be flushed out first.
No, because the system components are specifically made for use of one of them
Yes, if the aircraft is certified for the use of both types.

questions.questionText: A pressure regulator is used in a hydraulic system:
options: to ensure that an equal pressure flow is delivered to critical components such as servo actuators.
in conjunction with a variable delivery type pump.
as an interface between the system and the cockpit indicators.
in conjunction with a constant delivery type pump.

questions.questionText: The 'outflow valve' in a pressurization system maintains cabin altitude by:
options: regulating the amount of air escaping from the cabin.
changing the speed of the engine compressor.
adjusting the mix of bleed air and outside air.

questions.questionText: 130/934 Refer to figure 021-21. The hydraulic system that works correctly is shown in:
131/934 Shuttle valves will automatically:
options: switch hydraulically operated units to the most appropriate pressure supply.
shut down systems which are overloaded.

questions.questionText: An aeroplane equipped with fully powered flight controls (irreversible type):
options: does not require the use of an artificial feel system.
must be equipped with control locks
remains normally controllable in case of total loss of power to the flight control system.
requires the use of an artificial feel system.

questions.questionText: Which of these statements about rudder limiting are correct or incorrect? 1) A rudder ratio changer system reduces the rudder deflection for a given rudder pedal deflection as the IAS increases. 2) A variable stop system limits both rudder and rudder pedal deflection as the IAS increases:
PRIMARY flight controls are:
options: control wheel or stick, rudder pedals, flap lever and throttle
control wheel or stick, rudder pedals and speed brake

questions.questionText: A 'tandem' landing gear configuration is most commonly found on:
options: some high-wing cargo or glider aircraft.

questions.questionText: Trimming of aileron and rudder in an irreversible flight control system:
options: is achieved by adjusting the neutral point of the flight control actuator
is achieved by adjusting the "zero force point" of the feel system

questions.questionText: Which of these statements about a gust lock system are correct or incorrect? 1) There is no need for a gust lock on irreversible flight controls. 2) Manual flight controls should have a gust lock.
The expression "primary flight control" applies to the: 1) elevator, 2) speed brake, 3) lift-augmentation devices, 4) roll spoilers.

Which of these statements about rudder limiting are correct or incorrect? 1) A rudder ratio changer system limits both rudder and rudder pedal deflection as the IAS increases. 2) A variable stop system reduces the rudder deflection for a given rudder pedal deflection as the IAS increases.

Which of these statements about trimming in an irreversible flight control system of a conventional aeroplane are correct or incorrect? 1) The zero force position of the control column changes when using the pitch trim. 2) The zero force position of the control wheel changes when using the aileron trim.

Which of these statements about rudder limiting are correct or incorrect? 1) A rudder ratio changer system reduces the rudder deflection for a given rudder pedal deflection as the IAS decreases. 2) A variable stop system limits both rudder and rudder pedal deflection as the IAS increases.
The purpose of a 'shuttle valve' is to:
options: allow two different power sources to operate a single component.
reverse the direction of a hydraulic motor.
shut down the system if a leak is detected.

questions.questionText: H 56327): A flight control surface actuator is said to be "irreversible" when:
options: there is a need to lock the flight controls on the ground.
there is no feedback to the pilot's controls of the aerodynamic forces acting on the control surface
the flight control system has an alternate means of control in case of a control jam.
the pilot does not feel any force when moving that control surface in flight.

questions.questionText: An artificial feel unit is necessary in the pitch channel of a flight control system when:
options: the elevators are actuated by irreversible servo-control units
the elevators are actuated by reversible servo-control units.
the elevators are fitted with servo-tabs or trim tabs.

questions.questionText: Which of these statements about a gust lock system are correct or incorrect? 1) A gust lock can be used in flight to reduce the effects of turbulence. 2) There is no need for a gust lock on reversible flight controls.
Given a conventional transport aeroplane with irreversible flight controls on the ground with engines running. Which of these statements about rudder trim actuation is correct?
options: The "zero force point" of the artificial feel system changes, the rudder does not move.
The rudder moves, the rudder pedals move in the corresponding direction
The rudder trim tab moves and the rudder pedals do not move.
The rudder moves, the rudder pedals do not move.

questions.questionText: The function of the rudder limiter system is:
options: to reduce pilot's workload during engine failure.
to restrict the rudder deflection during flight at high altitudes.
to restrict rudder deflection during flight at high IAS
to limit pedal movement in heavy turbulence.

questions.questionText: What is the primary benefit of a multi-disc brake system?
options: It is lighter than a single disc system.
It provides a larger surface area for heat dissipation and friction.
It does not require hydraulic fluid.

questions.questionText: Rudder trim adjustment in an aeroplane with irreversible flight controls is:
options: an adjustment of the zero force rudder position
unnecessary because this aeroplane does not need rudder trim.
an adjustment of the rudder trim tab.
an adjustment of the rudder ratio changer.

questions.questionText: Most transport aeroplanes are provided with protection against control jamming. This means that:
options: seized brakes can be released from the cockpit.
the flight control system has provisions to disconnect the part of the control system that becomes blocked
the aeroplane is protected against the adverse effects of strong electromagnetic radiation.
in case of seizure, engine control is taken over automatically by an alternate electric circuit.

questions.questionText: An aeroplane equipped with reversible flight controls:
options: is equipped with simple spring type feel units.
need not be equipped with a separate gust lock system.
does not require an artificial feel system

questions.questionText: M32010): Which of these statements about a gust lock system are correct or incorrect? 1) There should be suitable design precautions to prevent flight with the gust lock engaged. 2) Reversible flight controls should have a gust lock.

Which of these statements about trimming in an irreversible flight control system of a conventional aeroplane are correct or incorrect? 1) The zero force position of the control column does not change when using the pitch trim. 2) The zero force position of the control wheel does not change when using the aileron trim.

options: using a hand crank connected to a gearbox.

questions.questionText: Given an aeroplane with irreversible primary flight controls, how is control maintained if one hydraulic system is lost due to a hydraulic leak?
options: Sufficient reserve hydraulic fluid is available to compensate the effects of the leak.
The remaining systems will take over control
By switching to manual back-up mode.
By switching the flight control system to the reversible mode.

questions.questionText: The expression "primary flight control" applies to the: 1) stabilizer, 2) rudder, 3) speed brake, 4) aileron.
An aeroplane equipped with irreversible flight controls:
options: does not require an artificial feel system.
need not be equipped with a separate gust lock system
must have a back-up control system.

questions.questionText: An artificial feel system:
options: functions in series with an irreversible hydraulic actuator.
is mounted in parallel with a spring tab.
functions in parallel with an irreversible hydraulic actuator

questions.questionText: The expression "secondary flight control" applies to the: 1) elevator, 2) speed brake, 3) lift-augmentation devices, 4) roll spoilers.

options: ensures that the (part of) slats are always extended when the ground/flight system is in the "ground" position.

provides for automatic slat retraction after take-off.

questions.questionText: In a pneumatic system, the 'pressure regulator' ensures that:
options: the system pressure remains at a constant, safe level.
the air is always at the same temperature.

questions.questionText: The expression "secondary flight control" applies to the: 1) trimmable stabilizer, 2) rudder, 3) speed brake, 4) aileron.
A Krueger flap is normally located at the:
options: trailing edge close to the wing root.
trailing edge close to the wing tip.

questions.questionText: The reason for a double switch on the elevator trim is:
options: because there are two trim motors.
to reduce the probability of a trim runaway
to be able to use two different trim speeds, slow trim rate at high speed and high trim rate at low speed.
to prevent both pilots from performing opposite trim inputs.

questions.questionText: The automatic ground spoiler extension system is normally activated during landing by:

options: a single wheel on a strut.
a pair or more of wheels attached to a single shock strut.
a gear that rotates 90 degrees during retraction.

questions.questionText: The advantages of fly-by-wire control are: 1) reduction of the electric and hydraulic power required to operate the control surfaces, 2) lesser sensitivity to lightning strike, 3) direct and indirect weight saving through simplification of systems, 4) immunity to different interfering signals, 5) improvement of piloting quality throughout the flight envelope.
Which modes of operation can be found in fly-by-wire aircraft?
options: Take-off/Go-around, Flight, Landing, and sometimes Taxi

questions.questionText: Modern fly-by-wire systems feature different modes of operation. Which one is the most basic?

options: Normal mode, Alternative (secondary) mode, Direct mode and Mechanical Backup
Normal mode, Alternative (secondary) mode and Direct mode
Alternative (secondary) mode, Direct mode and Mechanical Backup
Normal mode, Direct mode and Mechanical Backup

questions.questionText: Consider a piston engined aircraft. Which source is pneumatic air taken from?
The color of MIL-H-5606 hydraulic fluid is:

options: supplying the engine with high pressure airflow.

questions.questionText: Engine bleed air used for air conditioning and pressurization in turbo-jet aeroplanes is usually taken from the:
In flight, the cabin air for large jet transport aeroplanes is usually supplied by:
Cabin air for modern transport airplanes is usually taken from:
options: HP compressor and from the LP compressor if necessary

questions.questionText: The purpose of the "pressure relief valve" in a high pressure oxygen system is to:
options: relieve overpressure if the pressure reducing valve malfunctions
reduce pressure in the oxygen reservoir to a suitable manifold pressure for the regulator
maximize the charging pressure of the system.

questions.questionText: During level flight at a constant cabin pressure altitude (which could be decreased, even at this flight level), the cabin outflow valves are:
options: fully closed until the cabin descends to a selected altitude
fully closed until the cabin climbs to a selected altitude.
at the pre-set position for take-off.

questions.questionText: The color of Skydrol (phosphate ester) hydraulic fluid is typically:

options: cold air unit (air cycle machine) arrangement
cooling air across the inter-cooler heat exchanger.
means by which pressurization is controlled.

questions.questionText: In a pressurized aircraft whose cabin altitude is 6000 ft, a crack in a cabin window makes it necessary to reduce the differential pressure to 7 psi. The flight level to be maintained in order to keep the same cabin altitude is:

options: hot air extracted from the engine turbines
hot air extracted from the engine compressors

questions.questionText: On most large transport aeroplanes, the cabin pressure is controlled by regulating the:

options: there will be a constant air mass flow through the cabin
the pressurisation system must be controlled manually.
the pressurization system ceases to function until leakage reduces the pressure.
the outflow valves will move to the fully open position.

questions.questionText: In a 'constant pressure' hydraulic system, the pump is typically:
options: fixed displacement with a pressure regulator.

questions.questionText: In a typical air conditioning system of a short-haul turbojet aircraft the cabin air is not controlled in respect to the:
"Conditioned" air is air that has:
options: been controlled in respect of temperature and pressure
had any moisture removed from it.

questions.questionText: The maximum cabin differential pressure of an aeroplane with a maximum certified altitude of 41,000 ft is approximately:
Whilst in level cruising flight, an aeroplane with a pressurized cabin experiences a malfunction of the pressure controller. If the cabin vertical speed indicator reads 200 ft/min rate of descent:
options: the crew has to intermittently cut off the incoming air flow in order to maintain a zero cabin altitude.
a descent must be initiated to prevent the oxygen masks dropping when the cabin altitude reaches 14,000 ft.
the aircraft has to climb to a higher flight level in order to reduce the cabin altitude to its initial value.
the differential pressure will rise to its maximum value, thus causing the safety relief valves to open

questions.questionText: In a turbo compressor air conditioning system (bootstrap system), the purpose of the heat exchangers is to:
options: cool the bleed air in front and behind the compressor of the air cycle machine
allow a steady compressor outlet temperature.
cool bleed air before entering the complete pneumatic system.
allow a homogeneous temperature by mixing air flows from various air conditioning groups in operation.

questions.questionText: The 'scissor link' on a landing gear shock strut is another name for:

options: the cabin sensors only, modulating the mix valve.
the temperature selector only, modulating the mix valve.
the temperature selector in conjunction with cabin sensors and the temperature regulator, modulating the mix valve
automatic control of the ram air.

questions.questionText: The term "cabin altitude" refers to the:
options: flight level at which the aeroplane is flying.
difference in height between the cabin/floor and ceiling.

questions.questionText: Assuming cabin pressure decreases, the cabin rate of climb indicator should indicate:
options: a rate of descent dependent upon the cabin differential pressure.
a rate of descent of approximately 300 feet per minute.

questions.questionText: In a typical "bootstrap" cooling system the supply air is:
options: passed across an expansion turbine, then compressed and passed through a heat exchanger.
passed across an expansion turbine, then through a secondary heat exchanger and then across a compressor.
compressed, passed through a secondary heat exchanger, and then across an expansion turbine
passed across an expansion turbine, then directly to the heat exchanger.

questions.questionText: A turbo compressor air conditioning system (bootstrap system) includes two heat exchangers—the primary exchanger (P) and the secondary exchanger (S). The functions are:
options: P precools the engine bleed air; S cools air behind the pack's compressor
P pre-cools the engine bleed air; S increases the temperature of air for cargo compartment.
P warms up engine bleed air; S recirculates the cabin air.
P warms up engine bleed air; S increases the temperature of air from the pack compressor.

questions.questionText: Under normal conditions (EASA CS-25) the cabin pressure altitude of a transport aeroplane is not allowed to exceed:
Which component in a landing gear system absorbs the vertical energy of a landing?
Temperature regulation of an air conditioning system:
options: is manually controlled by the pilot reading the cabin temperature gauge.
is automatically controlled by sensing the output temperature from the control valve in relation to the temperature set on the pilot's control panel
is automatically controlled by sensing cabin temperature in relation to engine compressor outlet temperatures.

questions.questionText: The pack cooling fan provides:
options: cooling air to the primary and secondary heat exchanger during slow flight and ground operation
cooling air to the primary and secondary heat exchanger during cruise.
air to the eyeball outlets at the Passenger Service Unit (PSU).

questions.questionText: Assuming that during cruise flight with air conditioning packs ON, all the outflow valves fully close:
options: the air supply would automatically be stopped.
the cabin pressure would become equal to the ambient outside air pressure.
the skin of the cabin would be overstressed.
the pressure differential would go to the maximum value

questions.questionText: In a cabin air conditioning system with an air cycle machine (bootstrap system), the mass air flow is routed via the:
options: secondary heat exchanger outlet to the turbine inlet
secondary heat exchanger outlet to the compressor inlet.
compressor outlet to the primary heat exchanger inlet.
turbine outlet to the primary heat exchanger inlet.

questions.questionText: During a normal pressurized climb after take-off:
options: the cabin differential pressure is maintained constant.
cabin pressure decreases more slowly than atmospheric pressure
the pressurization system is inoperative until 10,000 ft is reached.
absolute cabin pressure increases to compensate for the fall in outside pressure.

questions.questionText: The 'ground-lock' pins are used to:
options: prevent accidental gear retraction while the aircraft is on the ground.
secure the aircraft to the hangar floor.

questions.questionText: A cabin pressure controller maintains a pre-set cabin altitude by regulating the:
options: mass air flow into the cabin.
position of the inward relief valves.
position of the duct relief valves.

questions.questionText: A turbo compressor air conditioning system (bootstrap system) will:
options: increase charge air pressure whilst causing the temperature to drop in the heat exchanger.
cause a pressure drop as well as an associated temperature drop in the charge air
not affect the charge air pressure.
decrease charge air pressure whilst causing the temperature to rise in the heat exchanger.

questions.questionText: The purpose of the cabin pressure controller operating in automatic mode is to: 1) control the cabin altitude, 2) control the cabin altitude rate of change, 3) limit the differential pressure, 4) balance the aircraft altitude with cabin altitude, 5) ventilate the cabin, 6) keep a constant differential pressure throughout all the phases of flight.
Cabin air for a large aeroplane during flight:
options: has a constant oxygen ratio regulated to a preset value.

questions.questionText: The cabin differential pressure is:
options: the pressure differential between the air entering and leaving the cabin.

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of 'venting' a fuel tank?
options: To allow air to enter as fuel is consumed, preventing a vacuum.
To let excess fuel vapor escape to prevent over-pressurization.
Both A and B are correct.
Neither A nor B is correct.

questions.questionText: The term "pressure cabin" applies when an aeroplane:
options: only pressurises the flight deck area.
has the ability to maintain a sea level cabin altitude at all altitudes.
has the ability to maintain a zero cabin differential pressure at all altitudes.
has the means to maintain cabin pressure higher than ambient pressure

questions.questionText: If the automatic control of an air conditioning system fails:
options: a red failure light will illuminate.
the pilot can revert to manual control and set the control valve to the required setting
the system must be shut down.
manual control can be selected but this is limited to "hot air" or "cold air" setting only.

questions.questionText: In a "bootstrap" cooling system, the bleed air is compressed in the cold air unit to:
options: ensure an adequate air flow across the secondary heat exchanger.
maintain a constant cabin mass air flow.
ensure a sufficient temperature drop in the secondary heat exchanger
increase the cabin air supply pressure when the inlet pressure is too low.

questions.questionText: If the pressure in the cabin tends to become lower than the outside ambient air pressure the:
options: negative pressure relief valves will close.
negative pressure relief valves will open

questions.questionText: Cabin differential pressure means the pressure difference between:
options: cabin pressure and ambient air pressure
cabin pressure and ambient air pressure at MSL.
actual cabin pressure and selected pressure.

questions.questionText: In an air cycle machine (bootstrap system), bleed air downstream of the first heat exchanger is:
options: passed across an expansion turbine, then compressed and passed through a secondary heat exchanger.
compressed, passed through a secondary heat exchanger, and then passed across an expansion turbine
passed across an expansion turbine, then directly passed to the second heat exchanger.
compressed, then passed across an expansion turbine and finally passed across the second heat exchanger.

questions.questionText: The 'primary' purpose of the landing gear is to:
options: provide a means of steering the aircraft.
support the aircraft on the ground and during takeoff/landing.

questions.questionText: Under normal flight conditions, cabin pressure is controlled by:
options: regulating the discharge of air through the outflow valves

questions.questionText: In a turbo compressor air conditioning system (bootstrap system), the main water separation unit is located:
Main cabin temperature is:
options: not controllable at the maximum cabin differential pressure.
only controllable at maximum cabin differential pressure.
controlled automatically, or by flight crew selection

questions.questionText: In an air cycle machine (bootstrap system) the:
options: turbine drives the compressor, which increases the temperature of the air, thus making the second heat exchanger more efficient.
turbine increases the pressure of the air supply to the cabin.
temperature drop across the turbine is the main contributor to the cooling effect of the air cycle machine
turbine drives the compressor, which provides pressurisation.

questions.questionText: At FL310, the cabin outflow valve moves to a completely open position, causing depressurisation. The crew don their oxygen masks and start an emergency descent. The following indications can be seen on the cabin VSI:
options: The cabin altitude increases until the pressure differential is zero, then the cabin VSI shows a descent equal to the aircraft VSI
The cabin altitude decreases more slowly until the pressure differential is zero.
The cabin VSI shows a climb until it indicates the altitude of the valve failure.
The cabin VSI shows a climb until cabin pressure equals ambient pressure.

questions.questionText: A 'retractable' landing gear is primarily used to:
options: increase the weight of the aircraft.
make the aircraft look more modern.

questions.questionText: In a typical air conditioning system of modern large transport aeroplanes the reduction of air temperature and pressure is achieved by:
Assuming cabin differential pressure has reached the required value in normal flight conditions, if flight altitude and air conditioning system setting are maintained:
options: the outflow valves will move to the fully open position.
the pressurization system ceases to function until leakage reduces the pressure.
the mass air flow through the cabin is constant
the outflow valves will move to the fully closed position.

questions.questionText: The purpose of an air conditioning pack inlet flow valve (pack valve) is to:
options: discharge cabin air to atmosphere if cabin pressure rises above the selected altitude.
regulate cabin pressure at the maximum cabin pressure differential.
regulate cabin pressure to the selected altitude.
maintain a constant and sufficient air mass flow to ventilate the cabin

questions.questionText: M 30575): A warning device alerts the crew in case of an excessive cabin altitude. This warning must be triggered on reaching the following altitude:
If the maximum certified altitude of an aeroplane is limited by the pressurised cabin, this limitation is due to the maximum:
options: positive cabin differential pressure at maximum cabin altitude
negative cabin differential pressure at maximum cabin altitude.

questions.questionText: The 'nose wheel steering' system is typically powered by:
When the air is compressed for pressurization purposes, the percentage oxygen content is:
options: dependent on the degree of pressurization.

questions.questionText: The primary function of an air cycle machine is to:
options: pump the conditioned air into the cabin.
decrease the pressure of the bleed air.
remove water from the bleed air.

questions.questionText: If the maximum operating altitude of an airplane is limited by the pressurized cabin, this limitation is due to the maximum:
options: negative cabin differential pressure at maximum operating ceiling.
positive cabin differential pressure at maximum cabin altitude
negative differential pressure at maximum cabin altitude.
positive cabin differential pressure at maximum operating ceiling.

questions.questionText: Cabin pressure is controlled by:
options: the cabin air mass flow control inlet valve(s).
delivering a substantially constant flow of air into the cabin and controlling the outflow
controlling the flow of air into the cabin with constant outflow.

questions.questionText: The purpose of the pack cooling fans in the air conditioning system is to:
options: supply the heat exchangers with cooling air during cruise flight.
supply the Passenger Service Unit (PSU) with fresh air.
supply the heat exchangers with cooling air during slow flights and ground operation

questions.questionText: A cabin rate of descent:
options: results in a cabin pressure decrease.
results in a cabin pressure increase
is always the same as the aeroplane's rate of descent.
is not possible at constant aeroplane altitudes.

questions.questionText: What happens if the 'anti-skid' system fails?
options: The brakes will not work at all.
Normal braking is still available, but without skid protection.
The landing gear will not retract.
The aircraft will automatically veer off the runway.

questions.questionText: An air cycle machine (air conditioning pack):
options: increases outlet pressure whilst causing the temperature to drop in the heat exchanger.
does not affect the bleed air.
decreases bleed air pressure whilst causing the temperature to rise in the heat exchanger.
causes a pressure and temperature drop in the bleed air

questions.questionText: An aeroplane with a pressurised cabin flies at FL310 and the outflow valve runs to the open position. This results in:
options: CAB V/S increase, CAB ALT increase, DELTA P decrease
CAB V/S decrease, CAB ALT decrease, DELTA P increase.
CAB V/S increase, CAB ALT increase, DELTA P increase.
CAB V/S decrease, CAB ALT increase, DELTA P decrease.

questions.questionText: In a large transport aeroplane the main temperature reduction of the conditioned air is achieved in:
If the cabin altitude rises (aircraft in level flight), the differential pressure:
options: may exceed the maximum permitted differential.

questions.questionText: Which of the following components of a turboprop aircraft are protected by an anti-icing system?
options: Wings, pitot tubes and static ports.
Engine inlets, pitot tubes and static ports

create the suction necessary to cause fuel to flow through the carburettor main jets.

questions.questionText: Question (N 55412): The dry sump oil system for a piston engine incorporates an oil tank with normally two stack pipes installed. Where can you find those stack pipes?
options: Both in the sides of the tank.

Both in the bottom of the tank.
Both in the top of the tank.

options: to operate the fuel control unit.
reduce internal friction and provide cooling.

the flow velocity inside the oil lines.
the tendency of a liquid or gas to resist flow.
the pressure resistance of an oil.

options: difference between the pressure pump pressure and the scavenge pump pressure.
pressure of the oil on the inlet side of the pressure pump.
pressure in the oil tank reservoir.
pressure of the oil on the outlet side of the pressure pump.

questions.questionText: Question (N 91452): The dry sump oil system for a piston engine incorporates an oil cooler that is fitted:
options: between the oil tank and the pressure pump.
after the pressure pump but before the oil passes through the engine.
after the oil has passed through the engine and before it enters the sump.
in the return line to the oil tank after the oil has passed through the scavenge pump.

options: will not operate on the right magneto.
will not operate on the left magneto.
cannot be started with the switch in the ON position.
cannot be shut down by turning the switch to the OFF position.

questions.questionText: An aircraft magneto is switched off by:

a wire from the magneto is in contact with a metallic part of the engine.
the grounding wire of one of the magnetos is broken.
there are local hot points in the engine (probably due to overheating of the cylinder heads).

questions.questionText: The 'Frequency' of an AC generator depends on:
options: the speed of rotation and the number of poles.

questions.questionText: Question (N 16804): A capacitor in parallel with breaker points:
options: permits arcing across the breaker points.
induces a very high current across the primary windings.
induces a very high current across the secondary windings.
prevents arcing across the breaker points.

questions.questionText: Question (N 34803): Ignition systems of a running piston engine receive electrical energy from:
Question (N 56281): The very rapid magnetic field changes (flux changes) in the primary coil of a magneto are accomplished by the:
options: distributor arm aligning with one of the high tension segments.
rotor turning past the position of maximum flux in the armature.

questions.questionText: Question (N 79810): A capacitor in parallel with breaker points:
options: induces a very high current across the primary windings.
induces a very high current across the secondary windings.
induces a very high voltage across the secondary windings.

questions.questionText: Question (N 81001): An impulse coupling is installed on a magneto of a piston engine to:
options: provide a stronger spark on top dead centre for engine starting.

questions.questionText: Question (N 15265): With an aircraft fitted with a fixed pitch propeller, during flight at normal cruising speed, one magneto fails completely. This will first cause:

options: gives a retarded spark at starting.
advances ignition timing and gives a hotter spark at starting.
gives an automatic spark increase during high speed operation.
reduces magneto speed during engine warm up.

options: connect the battery to the magneto.
connect the contact breaker and condenser in series with the primary coil.
connect the secondary coil to the distributor.
control the primary circuit of the magneto.

questions.questionText: Question (N 67721): The purpose of a distributor in the ignition system of a piston engine is to distribute:
options: primary current to the sparking plugs.
secondary current to the sparking plugs.

questions.questionText: Question (N 80421): In a piston engine, the operating principle of magnetos consists of:
options: obtaining a high amperage, low voltage current in order to generate the spark.
breaking the primary circuit in order to induce a low amperage, high voltage current, which is distributed to the spark plugs.
accumulating in a capacitor a low voltage current from the battery and inducing it as a high voltage current at the moment the spark is generated.
creating a brief high intensity magnetic field that will be sent through the distributor at the appropriate time.

questions.questionText: Question (N 5947): Under normal running conditions a magneto draws primary current:
options: from the aircraft batteries via an inverter.

questions.questionText: A 'continuous loop' fire detector works on the principle of:
options: measuring the brightness of the fire.
detecting a change in resistance or capacitance due to temperature.
the melting of a fuse link.

questions.questionText: Question (N 15076): Dual ignition provides a factor of reliability and:
options: saves wear caused by using one magneto constantly.

questions.questionText: Question (N 10598): An impulse coupling is inhibited at higher than normal rotational starting speeds by:
options: electro-magnetic action of an operating magneto.

questions.questionText: Question (N 24390): Once the engine has started, ignition systems of piston engines are:
options: independent of the electrical system of the aircraft.

questions.questionText: Question (N 10632): As altitude increases, if the mixture is not leaned:

the volume of air entering the carburettor decreases and the amount of fuel decreases.

it chemically interrupts the combustion process and is non-conductive.

questions.questionText: When excessively leaning the mixture for a better fuel economy, but still on the rich side of the peak EGT, the following engine parameter(s) may exceed their normal operating ranges:
options: low cylinder head and exhaust gas temperature.

options: both the density of air entering the carburettor and the fuel flow decrease.
the volume of air entering the carburettor remains constant and the fuel flow increases.
the mixture remains constant and the fuel flow decreases.
the density of air entering the carburettor decreases and the fuel flow increases.

questions.questionText: Question (N 31267): In a piston engine if the ratio of air to fuel is approximately 9:1, the mixture is:
Question (N 63788): A mixture setting richer than best power has to be used during climb segments. This results in a:
Question (N 52404): Maximum exhaust gas temperature (EGT) of a piston engine is theoretically associated with a:
options: mixture ratio very close to idle cut-out.

questions.questionText: On the diagram showing the power output of a piston engine as a function of mixture ratio (figure 021-12), theoretical best economy on the rich side of the peak EGT is shown at:

options: the aircraft is ready for flight.
the fire extinguisher has been discharged due to over-pressurization (thermal discharge).

questions.questionText: Question (N 42249): An EGT (Exhaust Gas Temperature) indicator for a piston engine is used to:
options: assist the pilot to set the correct mixture.

options: increase the fuel flow in order to compensate for the decreasing air pressure and density.

options: an increase of air density resulting in too lean a mixture.
a decrease in air density resulting in too rich a mixture.
a decrease of air density resulting in too lean a mixture.
an increase of air density resulting in too rich a mixture.

questions.questionText: For a piston engine, the chemically correct fuel/air ratio is:
Q579/934: When starting an engine or when the engine runs at an idle RPM on ground, the mixture is:

rich, to make starting possible and to cool the engine sufficiently when idling.
lean, to prevent that the engine consumes too much fuel.
rich, because carburettor heat is switched on.

questions.questionText: A 'smoke detector' in a cargo compartment often uses:

options: a decrease of air density with a fuel flow which becomes too low.

an increase of air density with a fuel flow which becomes too low.
an increase of air density with a fuel flow which becomes too high.

Q582/934: What may happen during a continuous climb with a mixture setting fully rich?
options: The engine will operate smoother even though fuel consumption is increased.

options: mass of fuel and volume of air entering the carburettor.

volume of fuel and volume of air entering the cylinder.
mass of fuel and mass of air entering the cylinder.

The purpose of 'scavenge pumps' in a fuel system is to:

towards the smallest blade angle by centrifugal twisting moment and/or spring force.
towards the highest blade angle by oil pressure created by the windmilling propeller.

options: maintains its maximum propeller efficiency over a wider range of airspeeds

options: reduce so that the engine RPM can increase.

To enable the engine RPM to be increased with an accompanying increase in propeller RPM.

questions.questionText: What is 'Fuel Jettisoning' used for?
options: To increase the weight for a smoother landing.
To reduce aircraft weight to the maximum landing weight in an emergency.

high airspeeds with low power setting.

high manifold pressure and low RPM.

Q611/934: When changing power on engines equipped with constant-speed propeller, engine overload is avoided by:

increasing the manifold pressure before increasing the RPM.

options: remove ice that has already accumulated.
increase the lift of the wing.

compressor driving a turbine via a reduction gear.
turbine driving a compressor via a reduction gear.

that there is no danger of detonation.
that there is no torsion at the crankshaft.

in level flight, manifold pressure will remain constant when the RPM is increased by opening the throttle.

the eye of the impeller and leaves it almost at a tangent to the periphery.

Q622/934: The power output of a piston engine without turbocharging increases with increasing altitude in standard atmosphere at constant MAP and RPM because of the:
options: increase of the air density behind the throttle valve.

options: prevent ice from blocking the pressure port.
increase the accuracy of the altimeter.

options: provide a richer mixture at high altitudes.

options: The blade angle may reach the full line limit.

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of a 'Weeping Wing' system (TKS)?
options: To wash the wings during flight.

To allow fuel to leak out in an emergency.

Q629/934: A turbocharger system in a reciprocating engine is normally driven by:
options: an electrically activated hydraulically powered clutch.

Q632/934: In a gas turbine engine, the maximum gas temperature is attained:

questions.questionText: Q638/934: Given the information about a turbojet engine: Net engine thrust equals:

questions.questionText: The purpose of a 'fuel-oil heat exchanger' is to:

questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements is correct about the flow in a divergent (subsonic) gas turbine engine intake?
1) The dynamic pressure decreases in the flow direction.
2) The static pressure increases in the flow direction.
767/934 Severe compressor stall can be indicated amongst others by:
options: an increase in engine pressure ratio (EPR) and a decrease in TGT and fuel flow.
a rise in TGT and an increase in fuel flow.
a decrease in TGT and a change in vibration level.
a rise in turbine gas temperature (TGT) and a change in vibration level.

questions.questionText: In a gas turbine engine, compressor blades, which are not rigidly fixed in position when the engine is stationary, take up a rigid position when the engine is running due to:
options: the resultant of aerodynamic and centrifugal forces.

questions.questionText: Q769/934 Creep of turbine blades is caused by:
options: bending stresses set up by gas pressure.
high blade temperature whilst under centrifugal loading.

questions.questionText: The primary danger of 'ice accumulation' on a wing is:
options: it makes the plane too heavy to fly.
it changes the aerodynamic shape, reducing lift and increasing stall speed.
it causes the engines to stop.

questions.questionText: Q770/934 One reason to fit a tip shroud to a turbine blade is to:
options: provide a seal for the cooling airflow.

questions.questionText: Q771/934 The function of the swirl vanes round the fuel nozzles is to: increase air pressure at the fuel nozzles. reduce the average axial flow speed in order to stabilise the flame front. generate a swirl of incoming air to enhance mixing of the fuel with air.

Which of these statements about an impulse turbine are correct or incorrect?
1) The pressure drops across the nozzle guide vanes.
2) The pressure remains constant across the rotor blades.
The compressor pressure ratio of an axial flow compressor is primarily determined by the:
774/934 On a FADEC gas turbine engine during normal flight, the power for the FADEC's sensors comes from:
options: the FADEC power source(s) on the engine.

questions.questionText: 775/934 Concerning the starting procedure of a gas turbine engine:
options: If the HP fuel valve is opened at a relatively high compressor rpm, this may result in a hot start.
If the air starter seizes, the engine can still be started by means of another air bleed source.
the selected igniters are activated before fuel is delivered to the fuel nozzles.
the higher the starter air pressure, the higher the EGT during starting.

questions.questionText: An 'Oxygen Candle' (chemical oxygen generator) produces oxygen by:
options: burning a chemical mixture (e.g., sodium chlorate).

questions.questionText: 776/934 The FADEC of a gas turbine engine normally includes:
options: an engine overspeed and/or an EGT protection function.
an overheat protection for the electronic engine control unit.
an excessive fuel flow detection function.

questions.questionText: 77/934 A magnetic plug in an engine oil system can be used to:
options: collect carbon found in the oil.
prevent metallic particles from entering the oil system.

questions.questionText: 778/934 In a gas turbine engine, the power changes are normally made by controlling the amount of:
options: air entering the compressor and fuel entering the combustion chambers.
air leaving the compressor by opening or closing of bleed valves.

questions.questionText: Q779/934 The purpose of a chip detector in the oil system of an engine/gearbox is to indicate that:
options: the oil temperature is too high.
there are metal particles in the oil.

questions.questionText: 780/934 The thrust reversers of jet engines may use clamshell doors in order to:
options: stop the flow of the exhaust gas stream.
change the direction of the exhaust gas stream.
absorb any surge in engine thrust.
reduce the pressure of the exhaust gas stream by diffusing it to atmosphere.

questions.questionText: The 'diluter-demand' oxygen regulator provides:
options: a mixture of air and oxygen based on altitude, or 100% oxygen if selected.
oxygen only when the pilot inhales.
oxygen at a constant flow rate.

questions.questionText: 781/934 A reverse thrust alert on the flight deck occurs when: the reverser doors are unlocked with the reverse lever in the stowed position. the reverser doors are locked with the reverse lever in the stowed position. the reverser doors remain stowed with the reverse lever in the deployed position. the reverser doors have moved to the reverse thrust position with the reverse lever in the deployed position.
782/934 Engine auxiliary gearboxes:
options: provide the drives for a wide variety of services and may even provide for the support of the engine in the airframe.
never provide a torque sensing facility.
are driven by the output from the free power turbine.
always provide drives for fuel pumps, oil pumps, speed sensing and torque sensing.

questions.questionText: 783/934 The pressurising function of the pressurising and dump valve in a gas turbine engine fuel system is:
options: to admit fuel to the nozzles only if the fuel pressure is high enough for sufficient atomisation.
to prevent fuel leakage in the combustion chamber after engine shutdown.
to prevent overpressure at the fuel nozzles.
to admit oil to the fuel-oil heat exchanger preventing ice crystals blocking the fuel lines.

questions.questionText: Q784/934 The FADEC (Full Authority Digital Engine Control) provides: engine automatic shut-down if maximum N1 is exceeded. engine automatic shut-down if maximum EGT is exceeded. automatic thrust rating control. fully automatic engine starting.
Q785/934 The fuel-oil heat exchanger:
options: cools the oil and heats the fuel.
cools both the oil and the fuel.

questions.questionText: In a 'pressure-demand' oxygen system:
options: oxygen is forced into the pilot's lungs under pressure at high altitudes.
the pilot must pump the oxygen manually.
the oxygen is stored at extremely high pressure.

questions.questionText: Q786/934 After a successful start of a high bypass turbofan engine, the air starter:
options: is disengaged by the coupling between engine N1 spool and starter by the use of centrifugal forces.
is disengaged by using a clutch that is controlled by the main engine control.
remains engaged and rotates continuously with the N2 spool.
is disengaged by the coupling between engine N2 spool and starter by the use of centrifugal forces.

questions.questionText: Q787/934 An aircraft flying in conditions of continuous heavy rain and high ambient temperatures may require the precautionary use of:
options: both engine intake anti-icing and airframe de-icing.

questions.questionText: 788/934 Engine oil reservoirs are sometimes located at the front of the engine:
options: to allow the hot oil to heat the air intake.
to utilise the structure necessary to contain the oil to form the engine accessory gearbox and provide mounting points for oil and fuel pumps.
because it is the most distant position from the exhaust.
to provide an attachment point for the mounting of the compressor and turbine bearings.

questions.questionText: 789/934 The reason for having a low pressure fuel-cooled oil cooler in a recirculatory type oil system is to:
options: cool the oil and heat the fuel.
cool both the oil and the fuel.

questions.questionText: 790/934 Fuel for gas turbine engines is usually heated in order to:
options: prevent icing-up of the LP filter.

questions.questionText: Q791/934 The use of igniters in a gas turbine engine is required: throughout the operating range of the engine during engine accelerations for ground starts for in-flight relights during turbulence in flight under heavy precipitation or in icing conditions
The purpose of a 'Mach Trim' system is to:
options: automatically adjust the pitch to prevent 'Mach Tuck' at high speeds.

questions.questionText: Q792/934 For a turbine engine, the self-sustaining speed relates to the minimum RPM at which the engine:
options: operates most efficiently in the cruise.
is designed to IDLE after starting.
will continue to operate without any starter motor assistance.
will enable the generators to supply bus-bar voltage.

questions.questionText: 793/934 The primary reason for venting an engine oil tank is to:
options: prevent excessive pressure in the tank.
allow for expansion of hot oil.

questions.questionText: 794/934 The meaning of abbreviation "FADEC" on a gas turbine engine is:
Q795/934 Concerning the fuel system high-pressure shut-off valve (HPSOV) of a gas turbine engine:
options: manual HPSOV control from the cockpit is not possible with FADEC equipped engines.
ignition is activated before fuel is delivered by the HPSOV to the fuel nozzles.
the HPSOV is mounted in the wing front spar near the pylon.
the HPSOV is located between the HP fuel pump and the fuel control unit.

questions.questionText: 797/934 In an ignition system during a normal start of a gas turbine engine, igniter activation:
options: should commence prior to starter motor engagement to prevent a hot start.
should commence prior to fuel entering the combustion chamber.
commences upon starter motor engagement, and is deactivated at a rotor speed of approximately 15% below idle rpm.
should commence after fuel has entered the combustion chamber.

questions.questionText: A 'Yaw Damper' is used to:
options: assist the pilot in steep turns.
lock the rudder on the ground.

questions.questionText: 798/934 The oil system of a gas turbine engine may be fitted with magnetic plugs, or chip detectors, that:
options: provide warning of impending failure without having to remove the filters for inspection.
monitor oil pressure and oil temperature.
are fitted in the pressure line upstream of the oil filter.
dispense with the requirement to fit an oil filter.

questions.questionText: If a gas turbine engine fuel/oil heat exchanger is located downstream of the HP fuel pump, internal leakage of the heat exchanger will cause the oil level to:
options: fall and the oil jets eventually to become clogged.
rise and the fuel nozzles eventually to become clogged.

questions.questionText: Q800/984 The purpose of magnetic chip detectors is to:
options: perform the function of a micron filter.
remove large items of debris from the system.
Increase lubricating oil adhesion to main surfaces.

questions.questionText: Q801/934 The electronic engine control unit of a gas turbine engine FADEC uses data from: aircraft systems. cockpit display units. its own engine sensors. electronic engine control units of the other engine(s).

802/934 The FADEC (Full Authority Digital Engine Control): provides fully automatic engine starting. modifies aircraft airspeed in order to allow for the lowest fuel consumption. counters any yaw movement in case of engine failure. provides thrust reverser control.
803/934 A FADEC on a gas turbine engine:
options: consists of two identical channels cross linked through an non engine related aircraft computer.

questions.questionText: The 'Fly-by-Wire' system replaces mechanical linkages with:
804/934 On most high by-pass ratio (fan) engines, reverse thrust is achieved by reversing:
options: only the direction of the fan airflow.
the direction of rotation of the fan.
only the direction of the exhaust gas flow.
the direction of both the exhaust gas flow (hot stream) and the fan airflow (cold stream).

questions.questionText: 805/934 The fuel/oil heat exchanger in a turbine engine uses:
options: engine oil and is connected to the high pressure area.
hydraulic fluid and is connected to the high pressure area.
hydraulic fluid and is connected to the low pressure area.
engine oil and is connected to the low pressure area.

questions.questionText: Q806/934 The FADEC (Full Authority Digital Engine Control) can provide: thrust reverser control. engine operation within safe limits. automatic engine starting sequence. automatic thrust rating control.
Q807/934 The Throttle Lever Angle (TLA) information of a FADEC controlled gas turbine engine is provided:
options: to the hydro mechanical main engine control.
to the electronic engine control unit by a direct electrical wiring connection.
to a single dedicated channel of the FADEC.

questions.questionText: 808/934 The FADEC of a gas turbine engine consists of:
options: an electronic engine control unit and the engine display units in the cockpit.
an electronic engine control unit only.
all electronic engine control units installed on the aircraft.
an electronic engine control unit and its peripherals (fuel metering, actuators, electrical wiring, sensors...).

questions.questionText: An 'Autothrottle' (or Autotrust) system:
options: automatically adjusts the engine power to maintain a set speed or thrust.
steers the aircraft on the ground.

questions.questionText: 809/934 Most high-bypass turbofan engines are equipped with a cold exhaust reverser (fan reverser) only in order to:
options: prevent extra fuel consumption due to operation of the hot exhaust reverser (turbine reverser).
prevent disturbance of cold exhaust reversed airflow by the hot exhaust reversed gas flow.
reduce the risk of exhaust gas re-ingestion.
save costs and mass at the expense of a reduction in available reverse thrust.

questions.questionText: 810/934 A Full Authority Digital Engine Control (FADEC) has the following functions: flow regulation (fuel, decelerations and accelerations monitoring). automatic starting sequence. transmissions of engine data to the pilot's instruments. thrust management and protection of operation limits. monitoring of the thrust reversers.
Q811/934 The electronic engine control of a FADEC gas turbine engine checks:
options: ACTIVE CHANNEL input and output data only.

questions.questionText: 817/934 If there is a single input data failure on a FADEC gas turbine engine:
options: the electronic engine control unit uses a constant data value for computation.
the engine is set at a fail-safe thrust.
the engine continues to operate normally.

questions.questionText: 813/934 Labyrinth seals in the lubrication system of a gas turbine engine are designed to provide a seal that is:
options: not completely tight, allowing some movement between rotating and static parts.
not completely tight, allowing only some radial movement between rotating and static parts.
completely tight, allowing no movement between rotating and static parts.
completely tight, allowing some axial movement between rotating and static parts.

questions.questionText: The 'Stick Shaker' is a device that:
options: indicates the engine is running roughly.
provides a tactile warning to the pilot that a stall is imminent.
helps the pilot move the controls.
signals that the autopilot has disconnected.

questions.questionText: Q814/934 The accessory units driven by the accessory gearbox of a turbo-jet engine are the: thrust reverser pneumatic motors. AC generator through its constant speed drive (CSD). oil pumps. hydraulic pumps. high pressure fuel pumps.
815/934 To achieve reverse thrust on a turbine engine with a high by-pass ratio, generally:
options: the flow of hot stream air is reversed.
the direction of rotation of the fan is reversed.

questions.questionText: 816/934 The function of a vibrator in some gas turbine ignition systems is to provide:
options: a DC current to the transformer to facilitate the generation of a higher voltage to the igniters.
an AC voltage in order to facilitate transformation to a higher voltage.
DC current if the ignition system is fed by AC voltage.
a pulsating AC voltage in order to generate a more powerful spark.

questions.questionText: Q817/934 The oil supply to gas turbine engine bearings is maintained in the event of the main oil filter becoming clogged by:
options: differential pressure opening the bypass valve of the main oil filter.

questions.questionText: Q818/934 A hung (abortive) start of a gas turbine engine may be caused by:
options: failure of the fuel to ignite in the starting sequence after the engine has been accelerated to the required rpm by the starter.
a too low pneumatic starter pressure.
the starter cutting out early in the starting sequence before the engine has accelerated to the required rpm for ignition.

questions.questionText: 819/934 A gas turbine engine is run at ground idle for a period of time prior to shutdown to:
options: prevent seizure of the engine bearings.
prevent vapour lock in the fuel control and/or fuel lines.
prevent seizure of the rotor blades in their seals.
allow the engine oil to cool.

questions.questionText: A 'Stick Pusher' system:
options: assists the pilot in pulling up.
automatically moves the control column forward to prevent a deep stall.
locks the controls on the ground.

questions.questionText: 820/934 The following problems may occur when starting a twin-spool turbofan engine:
options: no N2 rotation, although N1 may accelerate normally.
no N1 rotation, although N2 may accelerate normally.
a hung (abortive) start with no light-up indication after 'fuel-on' selection.
a hung (abortive) start with fuel flow indication zero after 'fuel on' selection.

questions.questionText: With regard to a twin spool turbofan, an abortive start (hung start) will result in:
options: N2 speed stabilizing at a lower than normal RPM and a subsequent increase in EGT.
N1 speed stabilizing at a higher than normal RPM.
Immediate engine flameout without temperature increase.
N2 speed exceeding the limit while EGT remains low.

questions.questionText: speed stabilising at a higher than normal RPM and a subsequent increase in EGT. speed stabilising at a lower than normal RPM and a subsequent decrease in EGT. speed stabilising at a higher than normal RPM and a subsequent decrease in EGT. An abortive (hung) start is the term used to describe an attempt to start in which the engine:
options: commences initial rotation, but fails to receive any fuel.
lights up, but fails to accelerate sufficiently.

questions.questionText: Q823/984 Which of the jet engine ratings below is not a certified rating?
824/934 The purpose of gas turbine engine trending is:
options: to assess engine condition using non-destructive inspection.
to enhance maintenance planning and detect anomalies.
to obtain a timely indication when an engine requires to be shut down due to problems.
to collect metal particles from the oil system.

questions.questionText: The 'EICAS' or 'ECAM' system provides:
options: integrated monitoring of engine and system parameters and alerts.

questions.questionText: Q825/934 The control of free turbine engines on turboprops, is accomplished by: PROPELLER control lever used to select: propeller RPM turbine temperature turbine RPM FUEL control lever used to select: propeller RPM torque turbine temperature
Q826/934 If the main oil filter of a gas turbine engine becomes clogged, oil to the main bearings is still assured:
options: if a higher engine RPM is selected to increase oil pressure.
if breather pressure in the main compartments is increased to ensure positive lubrication of the bearings.
because fuel upstream of the oil filter will be mixed with oil to allow easier passage through the filter.
because the oil filter bypass valve opens.

questions.questionText: 827/934 The primary performance indicator (cockpit instrument) for the thrust of a turbofan engine is:

Which statement is correct for a gas turbine engine with a constant speed propeller and free power turbine, when the power setting is decreased?
1) The HP spool speed decreases.
2) The EGT decreases.

Which statement is correct for a gas turbine engine with a constant speed propeller and free power turbine, when the power setting is increased?
1) The gas generator speed remains constant.
2) The EGT decreases.
What is the function of a 'Vortex Generator'?
options: To delay airflow separation at high angles of attack or high speeds.

questions.questionText: Exhaust Gas Temperature (EGT) sensing is usually taken:
options: between the stages of the gas generator turbine.
at the outlet from the combustion chamber.
between the gas generator turbine and the free power turbine.

questions.questionText: 831/934 In very cold weather, a slightly higher than normal engine oil pressure during startup:
options: is unacceptable but does not require the engine to be shut down.
is unacceptable and requires the engine to be shut down.
is acceptable, if it decreases after startup.

questions.questionText: 832/934 On landing and prior to shutdown, a gas turbine engine should be run at "ground idle" for a short period. This allows:
options: cool oil flushing of bearings, particularly those in the turbines.
the turbine blade temperature to reduce only.
the engine to be compressor washed.

questions.questionText: Q833/934 Vibration sensors are used in a gas turbine engine to indicate:
options: imbalance of one or both of the spools.
that there is too much free movement of the engine strut (pylon).
that tip clearance of turbine blades has decreased below a minimum value.

questions.questionText:
Which statement is correct for a gas turbine engine with a constant speed propeller and free power turbine, when the power setting is increased?
1) The HP spool speed decreases.
2) The EGT increases.
One of the causes of high oil temperature in a gas turbine engine at constant power setting is:
options: unusually high scavenge pump oil flow.

questions.questionText: The 'Spoiler' on a wing can be used for:
options: dumping lift on landing (as ground spoilers).

questions.questionText: If a twin-spool gas turbine engine is controlled by a conventional N2 control system, rupture of the LP shaft may result in:
options: Automatic shutdown of the engine by FADEC.

questions.questionText: and EPR increase. and N2 increase. decrease and EPR increase. Increasing the power setting of a gas turbine engine with a constant speed propeller and free power turbine causes:
options: HP spool speed to remain constant and EGT to increase.
both HP spool speed and EGT to increase.
HP spool speed to increase and EGT to remain constant.
HP spool speed to decrease and EGT to increase.

questions.questionText: 838/934 A pressure relief valve in an oil system that does not seat correctly would result in:

Which statement is correct for a gas turbine engine with a constant speed propeller and free power turbine, when the power setting is increased?
1) The gas generator speed increases.
2) The EGT increases.
When a turbo-prop aeroplane decelerates during the initial part of the landing roll, a constant speed propeller within the beta range:
Which type of flap extends rearward and downward, increasing both wing area and camber?
Q841/934 The most important oil system instrument indication during gas turbine engine start is:

Which statement is correct for a gas turbine engine with a constant speed propeller and free power turbine, when the power setting is increased?
1) The gas generator speed decreases.
2) The EGT remains constant.

Which statement is correct for a gas turbine engine with a constant speed propeller and free power turbine, when the power setting is increased?
1) The HP spool speed increases.
2) The EGT remains constant.
Q844/934 What is described by the term alpha range when talking about a turboprop?
Q845/934 If an abortive start (hung start) occurs when starting a gas turbine engine, the correct action is to:
options: move the thrust lever forward to increase fuel flow.

questions.questionText: A 'Slotted' flap allows high-pressure air from the lower surface to:
options: flow over the upper surface to delay airflow separation.

questions.questionText: 846/934 Consider the variable-pitch propeller of a turbo-prop aircraft. During deceleration:
options: when braking, the propeller supplies negative thrust and absorbs engine power.
at zero power, the propeller thrust is zero and the engine power absorbed is nil.
when feathered, the propeller produces thrust and absorbs no engine power.
with propeller windmilling, the thrust is zero and the propeller supplies engine power.

questions.questionText: If air is tapped from a gas turbine HP compressor (engine N2 controlled), the effect on the engine pressure ratio (EPR) and the exhaust gas temperature (EGT) is that:
options: EPR decreases and EGT remains constant.
EPR remains constant and EGT increases.

questions.questionText: 848/934 A flat rated turbofan will be temperature limited if:
options: ambient pressure is lower than standard sea level ambient pressure.
OAT is lower than flat rated temperature.
ambient pressure is higher than standard sea level ambient pressure.
OAT is higher than flat rated temperature.

questions.questionText:
Regarding a jet engine:
1) The maximum thrust increases as the pressure altitude decreases.
2) The specific fuel consumption (SFC) is proportional to pressure altitude, at constant TAS.
At constant fuel flow, if engine compressor air is bled off for engine anti-icing or a similar system, the turbine temperature (EGT):
options: may rise or fall depending on compressor stage.

questions.questionText: Bleeding air from the HP compressor of a turbofan, while maintaining thrust, increases:
options: HP compressor discharge pressure and N2.

questions.questionText: The 'Slat' is a leading-edge device that:
options: creates a slot to allow air to flow over the wing at high angles of attack.
protects the wing from bird strikes.
is only used on supersonic planes.

questions.questionText: Q852/934 Derated or reduced thrust take offs save engine life primarily due to:
options: reduced creep damage due to higher EGT levels during take- off.
the maximum oil temperature not being exceeded.
reduced corrosion of the HP turbine blades.

questions.questionText:
Regarding a jet engine:
1) The maximum thrust decreases as the pressure altitude increases.
2) The specific fuel consumption is inversely proportional to pressure altitude, at constant TAS.
The take-off of a jet aeroplane may be performed with "reduced thrust" (Flex Temp) in order to:
options: Reduce maintenance costs and engine wear.
Save a significant amount of fuel.
Reduce the risk of engine failure during takeoff.

questions.questionText: (N° 62697) Flat rated jet engines are designed to provide constant maximum take-off thrust up to a certain level of:
(N° 92005) A flat rated turbofan engine has a constant maximum rated thrust level:
options: up to a specific pressure altitude.

questions.questionText: The 'Stabilator' is a flight control surface that combines the functions of:
options: the horizontal stabilizer and the elevator.

questions.questionText: (N° 17352) Consider a jet engine whose control is based on the engine pressure ratio (EPR): with a constant EPR, the thrust decreases when the altitude increases. with a constant EPR, the thrust is independent of the Mach number. at same environmental conditions, a given EPR setting maintains the thrust irrespective of engine wear due to ageing. the EPR is determined by the total pressure difference between the turbine outlet and the compressor inlet. on take-off, in the event of icing not detected by the crew, the indicated EPR is lower than the real EPR. The combination regrouping all the correct statements is:
(N° 20118) Concerning the flexible take-off mode, the temperature selected in the FMS is:
options: lower than the ambient airfield temperature, in order to achieve an increased power setting.
higher than the ambient airfield temperature, in order to achieve an increased power setting.
lower than the ambient airfield temperature, in order to achieve a reduced power setting.
higher than the ambient airfield temperature, in order to achieve a reduced power setting.

questions.questionText: (N° 11337) The flexible take-off mode: can be used only if the engines are new or recently overhauled. reduces engine wear. can be used in situations where take-off can be executed without the need for full engine power. can only be used with an autothrottle. The combination that regroups all of the correct statements is:

(N° 74951) On the ground, the Auxiliary Power Unit (APU) can provide: electricity. air for main engine starting. hydraulic pressure. air for air conditioning. The combination that regroups all of the correct statements is:

(N° 87210) A modern auxiliary power unit (APU) is designed to provide power for ground starting of an engine. It also supplies both in the air (subject of certification limitations) and on the ground:
options: either air conditioning or electrical services, but never both at the same time.
air conditioning and thrust in the event of engine failure.
air conditioning and electrical services (on the ground) electrical and hydraulic back-up services (in the air).

questions.questionText: The 'V-tail' (Butterfly tail) arrangement uses 'Ruddervators' to control:
(N° 97158) The Auxiliary Power Unit (APU) has its own AC generator which:
options: must have the same characteristics as the main AC generator so that it can be easily coupled with the latter.
is driven at constant speed through a Constant Speed Drive (CSD) in the same way as the main AC generator.
supplies the aircraft with three-phase 115 V, 400 Hz AC.
is excited by its Generator Control Unit (GCU) as soon as the APU starts up.

questions.questionText: (N° 73756) What would cause an APU to automatically shut-down? High oil pressure Low oil pressure High EGT Low APU speed Overspeed APU fire The correct combination is:
(N° 16542) Regarding detection systems, ion detectors are used to detect:
(N° 64927) Smoke detector systems are installed in the:
(N° 25307) Photoelectric cells:
options: are smoke detectors and detect the diffusion of a beam of light which occurs when the beam is interrupted by smoke. The scattering of the light increases the conductance of the cell and its output is amplified to operate a warning circuit.
are visual smoke detectors which are only fitted as alarm verification devices.
are carbon-monoxide detectors which are found mainly in aircraft of American manufacture. These devices detect concentrations of CO and activate a warning system.
are alpha particle detectors and consist of ionisation chambers which measure alpha radiation from radium.

questions.questionText: (N° 54855) Smoke detectors fitted on transport aircraft are of the following type:
A 'Servo Tab' is used to:
options: assist the pilot in moving a large or heavy control surface.

questions.questionText: (N° 46345) How do smoke detectors operate?
options: A labyrinth optical detector - light refracts away from the photosensitive cell.
A light attenuation optical detector - light refracts towards the photosensitive cell.
A labyrinth optical detector - the photosensitive cell is blocked from the light source by smoke.
A light attenuation optical detector light refracts away from the photosensitive cell.

questions.questionText: (N° 8224) An engine fire detection system of a large multi-engine transport aircraft typically includes:
options: a warning light for each engine and a single aural alert common to all engines.
one warning light and one aural alert common to all engines.
a warning light common to all engines but a separate aural alert for each engine.
both a warning light and an aural alert unique to each engine.

questions.questionText: (N° 87422) A fault protection circuit in a fire detection system will:
options: automatically initiate APU shutdown and fire extinguisher striker activation in the event of fire.
inhibit the fire detector when the detection line is connected to ground.
activate the fire detection system when the detection line is connected to ground.
activate an alarm in the cockpit and in the landing gear bay for ground crew.

questions.questionText: (N° 96653) Explain the working principle of a gaseous fire detection system:
options: A closed tube, when heated, causes the gas inside to expand and trigger an alarm via a pressure switch.
A closed tube consists of a gas which changes its translucence when heated. Light sensors will sense such change in translucence and trigger an alarm.
A closed tube, when heated, causes the gas inside to become a conductor and thereby closes an alarm circuit.
A closed tube, when heated, causes the gas inside to contract which causes a negative pressure and thereby trigger an alarm via a pressure switch.

questions.questionText: (N° 56653) An engine fire in a large transport aircraft is indicated by a:
options: visual warning and an aural alert.

questions.questionText: An 'Anti-Servo Tab' on a stabilator moves in:
options: the opposite direction to the surface, making it easier to move.
the same direction as the surface, increasing the aerodynamic feel and stability.

questions.questionText: (N° 23457) When a part of a continuous loop fire detection system is heated: its resistance decreases. its resistance increases. the leakage current increases. the leakage current decreases. The combination regrouping all the correct statements is:
(N° 68699) The most common extinguishing agent used in gas turbine engine fire protection system is:
(N° 3163) Halon is used as a fire extinguishing agent because it:
options: acts as a very effective flame inhibitor.
uses the cooling effect created by the venturi during discharge.

questions.questionText: (N° 70133) In a fire detection system with single-loop continuous components (with no fault protection), if the inner core contacts the outer casing:
options: the engine fire extinguisher striker is automatically activated
the power supply is cut off automatically.
there will be no effect on the system.

questions.questionText: (N° 57020) In a fire detection system with single loop continuous components, with no fault protection, the sensing element is crushed, but the inner core does not touch the outer casing:
options: The fire extinguisher is automatically activated.
The power supply is automatically cut off.
The fire alarm will be triggered.

questions.questionText: The 'Balance Tab' moves in:
options: the same direction as the control surface.
the opposite direction to the control surface, reducing the force required by the pilot.
