What does the term "EXPECTED APPROACH TIME" mean?
options: The holding time over the radio facility from which the instruments approach procedure for a landing will be initiated.
The time at which ATC expects that an arriving aircraft, following a delay, will leave the holding point to complete its approach for a landing.
The time at which an arriving aircraft, upon reaching the radio aid serving the destination aerodrome, will commence the instrument approach procedure for a landing.
The time at which an arriving aircraft expects to arrive over the appropriate designated navigation aid serving the destination aerodrome.

questions.questionText: What does the term "BROADCAST" mean?
options: A transmission of information relating to air navigation that is not addressed to a specific station or stations.
A radiotelephony transmission from ground station to aircraft in flight.
A transmission containing meteorological and operational information to aircraft engaged in flights over remote and oceanic areas out of range of VHF ground stations.
A transmission where no reply is required from the receiving station.

questions.questionText: What does the term "CLEARANCE LIMIT" mean?
options: The time of expiry of air traffic control clearance.
The time after which air traffic control clearance will be automatically cancelled if the flight has not been commenced.
The time at which aircraft is given an air traffic control clearance.
The point to which aircraft is granted an air traffic control clearance.

questions.questionText: What does the term "BLIND TRANSMISSION" mean?
options: A transmission of information relating to air navigation that is not addressed to a specific station or stations.
A transmission from one station to another station in circumstances where two-way communication cannot be established but it is believed that the called station is able to receive the transmission.
A transmission where no reply is required from the receiving station.
A transmission of messages relating to enroute weather information which may affect the safety of aircraft operations that is not addressed to a specific station or stations.

questions.questionText: What does the abbreviation 'APV' mean?
options: aerodrome precision visibility (monitoring system by observation)

questions.questionText: ICAO defines the term instrument approach as follows:
options: From the beginning of a defined arrival route to a point from which a landing can be completed and thereafter, if a landing is not completed, to a position at which holding or en-route obstacle clearance criteria apply.
From the initial approach fix to a point from which a landing can be completed, e.g. MDA or DA.
From the beginning of a defined arrival route to a point from which a landing can be completed.

From the beginning of a defined approach to a point from which a landing can be completed and thereafter, if a landing is not completed, to an altitude of 1 500 ft above the aerodrome reference point.

questions.questionText: When flying in accordance with IFR, which of the following best describes the term "VISUAL APPROACH"?
options: An approach executed by an IFR flight unable to maintain VMC.
A visual manoeuvre executed by an IFR flight when the weather conditions at the aerodrome of destination are equal to or better than required VMC minima.
An approach by an IFR flight when either part or all of an instrument approach procedure is not completed and the approach is executed in visual reference to terrain.
An extension of an instrument approach procedure to bring an aircraft into position for landing on a runway which is not suitably located for straight-in-approach.

questions.questionText: What rate of speech does ICAO recommend for radio communications? An even rate of speech not exceeding:
Which of the following calls is a "GENERAL CALL"?
What does the term "AIR-GROUND COMMUNICATION" mean?
options: Two-way communication between aircraft and stations or locations on the surface of the Earth.
Any communication from aircraft to ground station requiring handling by the Aeronautical Fixed Telecommunication Network (AFTN).
One-way communication from stations or locations on the surface of the Earth.
One-way communication from aircraft to stations or locations on the surface of the Earth.

questions.questionText: What does the term "WAYPOINT" mean?
options: A specified geographical position used to define an area navigation route or the flight path of an aircraft employing area navigation.
A general term meaning the taxiway and the runway system of an international airport.
A signal indicating the direction of the runway-in-use.
A defined position on an aerodrome used for the calibration of the inertial navigation system.

questions.questionText: What does the abbreviation "MLS" mean?
What does "SELCAL" mean?
options: A system provided for direct exchange of information between air traffic services (ATS) units.
A system in which radiotelephony communication between two stations can take place in both directions simultaneously.
A system which permits the selective calling of individual aircraft over radiotelephone channels linking a ground station with the aircraft.
A system in which radiotelephony communication can be established between aircraft only.

questions.questionText: What does the abbreviation "INS" mean?
What does the abbreviation "RVR" mean?

What does the abbreviation "H24" mean?
options: Aircraft handling available 24 hours a day.

options: True heading to the station (no wind).

questions.questionText: What does QDR mean?
options: Magnetic heading to the station (no wind).

questions.questionText: What does QDM mean?
options: True heading to the station (no wind).
Magnetic heading to the station (no wind).

the altimeter sub-scale setting to obtain elevation when on the ground.
the atmospheric pressure measured at the aerodrome reference point (ARP).
the atmospheric pressure referred to the highest obstacle located on the surface of an aerodrome.

questions.questionText: QFE is the RTF Q-code to indicate:
options: the atmospheric pressure referred to the highest fixed obstacle located on the surface of an aerodrome.
the atmospheric pressure corrected to the aircraft cockpit height.
the atmospheric pressure at aerodrome elevation (or at runway threshold).
the altimeter sub-scale setting to obtain the flight level reference datum.

questions.questionText: Which of the following statements is correct?
options: There is no difference regarding priority between distress communications and urgency communications.
ATC clearances have the same priority as urgency communications.
The urgency communications have priority over all the other communications, except distress.
The urgency communications have priority over all the other communications.

options: flight safety message, meteorological message, flight regularity message.

questions.questionText: Flight safety messages are:
options: messages concerning the safety of an aircraft, a vessel, any other vehicle or a person.

options: the term DECIMAL must always be transmitted.
the term DECIMAL can be omitted with friendly ATS units only.
the term DECIMAL can be omitted if no chance of misunderstanding exists.
the term DECIMAL must be spoken only if followed by three digits.

questions.questionText: During radar vectoring, the controller asks the pilot of an aircraft XY-ABC to turn on to heading 360°. The correct readback of this instruction is:
options: There is no need to respond as you have been identified as XY-ABC.
Heading three hundred and sixty, X-BC.

questions.questionText: What shall the pilot read back for ATC instruction "CLIMB ALTITUDE 2 500 FEET"?
options: Climbing to two thousand five hundred.
Climbing altitude two thousand five hundred feet.
Up to two thousand five hundred.

questions.questionText: What is the correct way of transmitting 1001 as a QNH?
Regardless of your position, time figures are transmitted with reference to:
The time is 9:20 AM. What is the correct way of transmitting this time if there is no possibility of confusion about the hour?
The time is 4:15 PM. What is the correct way of transmitting this time if there is any possibility of confusion about the hour?
When transmitting time, which time system shall be used?
options: No specific system, as only the minutes are normally required.

questions.questionText: What is meant by good microphone technique?
options: Use a normal conversation tone, speak clearly and distinctly.
Speak very loudly into the microphone.
Make large use of hesitation sounds as ER.
Keep the microphone far away since it improves the readability.

questions.questionText: My message will be more effective and understandable if I:
options: maintain the speaking volume at a constant level.

questions.questionText: Before transmitting the pilot should:
options: always write the message and read it during the transmission.
make sure that the aircraft is levelled off.
listen out on the frequency to ensure no interference with another station already transmitting will occur.
make sure that the emergency frequency is tuned in at the same time.

questions.questionText: In the event that a pilot is required to make a "BLIND" transmission, this should be made:
options: only once on the designated frequency.

questions.questionText: What is the consequence of a microphone button stuck on transmit (switched on)?
options: Readability will improve for all stations.
The frequency can not be used by others.
Other stations will have to use the WORD TWICE technique.

questions.questionText: A "BLIND" transmission shall be made:
options: on the designated frequency (frequency in use).

questions.questionText: When an aeronautical station broadcasts information to more than one station, the call starts with:
options: MESSAGE TO ALL AIRCRAFT ON THIS FREQUENCY.

questions.questionText: What does the word "NEGATIVE" mean?
options: Consider that transmission as not sent.

questions.questionText: What does the phrase "BREAK BREAK" mean?
options: The exchange of transmissions has ended and no response is expected.
My transmission has ended and I expect a response from you.
It indicates the separation between messages transmitted to different aircraft in a very busy environment.
It indicates the separation between portions of a message transmitted to an aircraft station.

questions.questionText: What does the word "APPROVED" mean?
options: I repeat for clarity or emphasis.
Authorized to proceed under the conditions specified.

questions.questionText: Which word or phrase shall be used in order to repeat for clarity or emphasis?
ATC clears FASTAIR 345 to descend from FL100 to FL80. What is the correct read back by the pilot?
options: Leaving flight level 100 descending to flight level 80, FASTAIR 345.

questions.questionText: What does the word "DISREGARD" mean?
options: An error has been made in this transmission.
Consider that transmission as not sent.
Wait and I will call you.

questions.questionText: The Air Traffic Control unit has reported "radar contact". What does that mean to the pilot?
options: The aircraft is subject to positive control.
The radar identity of the aircraft has been established.
The pilot does not have to follow up the position of the aircraft.

questions.questionText: Which word or phrase shall be used if you want to say "REDUCE YOUR RATE OF SPEECH"?
You are making an approach to land, at what range would you make the call "SHORT FINAL"?
What does the word "RECLEARED" mean?
options: A change has been made to your last clearance.
Consider that transmission as not sent.
An error has been made in my last transmission.

questions.questionText: What does the word "CANCEL" mean?
options: Wait and I will call you.
Consider that transmission as not sent.
A change has been made to your last clearance.

questions.questionText: Which phrase shall be used if you want to say "YES"?
What does the word "CONTACT" mean?
How shall a pilot inform the control tower that he has to abandon the take-off manoeuvre?
What does the word "CORRECT" mean?
options: Permission for proposed action not granted.
An error has been made in this transmission. The correct version is...

questions.questionText: What does the word "ACKNOWLEDGE" mean?
options: Let me know that you have received and understood this message.
Repeat all of your last transmission.
Repeat all of this message back to me exactly as received.

questions.questionText: If you are flying an aircraft with a maximum rate of climb of 1 500 ft per minute and ATC asks you to climb at 2000 ft/min or greater, your answer will be:
Which word or phrase shall be used to indicate that a change has been made to your last clearance and this new clearance supersedes your previous clearance or part thereof?

options: Fastair 345 should listen on frequency 118.9 on which TOWER will initiate further communications.
Fastair 345 should standby on the current frequency.
Fastair 345 should change frequency to 118.9, on which aerodrome data are being broadcast.

questions.questionText: Which phrase should a pilot use to inform ATC that he is initiating a missed approach procedure?

Which elements of a position report cannot be omitted?

options: Wait and I will call you.

questions.questionText: When shall the phrase "TAKEOFF" be used by a pilot?
options: To inform TOWER when ready for departure.
Only when the aircraft has already moved onto the active runway.

questions.questionText: Which word shall be used to ask a station whether you have correctly received a message, clearance, instruction, etc?

To indicate that he is no longer occupying the active runway a pilot shall report to the ATC controller.

options: FASTBIRD 166, CLEARED TO LAND RUNWAY 24, WIND 270 DEGREES 15 KNOTS.
FASTBIRD 166, ILS APPROACH TO RUNWAY 24, CLEARED TO LAND.
FASTBIRD 166, REPORT RUNWAY 24 IN SIGHT, WIND 270 DEGREES 15 KNOTS.
FASTBIRD 166, REPORT FINAL RUNWAY 24, WIND 360 DEGREES 18 KNOTS.

questions.questionText: ATC reports traffic. Which phrase should be used if a pilot wants to inform ATC that he/she can see the corresponding traffic?
Which phrase shall be used if you want to say "COMMUNICATION IS DIFFICULT. PLEASE SEND EVERY WORD OR GROUP OF WORD TWICE"?
If you are requested to "REPORT FLIGHT CONDITIONS", what does that mean?
options: Indicate weather conditions as wind, visibility, temperature.
Indicate whether you are flying in IMC or in VMC..
Indicate if visibility is sufficient for landing
Indicate whether you are flying IFR or VFR.

questions.questionText: Which word or phrase shall be used when giving authorization to proceed under specified conditions?
What does the word "WILCO" mean?
options: I have received all of your last transmission.
I will call you later - communication is difficult.
I understand your message and will comply with it.

questions.questionText: Which word shall be used to indicate that an error has been made in a transmission or message?
If ATC asks aircraft D-ENRW to standby, what is the pilot expected to reply, if any?
options: No reply necessary, ATC will call back

questions.questionText: What does the word "CHECK" mean?
What is the radiotelephony call sign for the aeronautical station indicating approach control radar departures?
What is the radiotelephony call sign for the aeronautical station providing surface movement control of aircraft on the manoeuvring area?
What is the radiotelephony call sign for the aeronautical station providing approach control (no radar service)?
What is the radiotelephony call sign suffix for the aeronautical station indicating aerodrome control service?
What is the radiotelephony call sign suffix for the aeronautical station indicating clearance delivery?
What is the radiotelephony call sign for the aeronautical station indicating Area Control Centre (no radar)?
What is the radiotelephony call sign suffix for the aeronautical station providing radar service (in general)?
What is the radiotelephony call sign for the aeronautical station indicating approach control radar arrivals?
An Airbus 321 registered as G-HBTD has call sign "Sun Air HBTD". What is its abbreviated call sign?
When shall an aircraft in the heavy wake turbulence category include the word HEAVY immediately after its call sign e.g. FASTAIR 345 HEAVY?
options: in all calls to the aerodrome tower and the approach control unit.
in the initial call to the aerodrome control tower and the approach control unit.

questions.questionText: When is an aircraft station allowed to use its abbreviated call sign?
options: After it has been addressed in this manner by the aeronautical ground station.
Only after satisfactory communication has been established.
Provided no confusion is likely to result.

questions.questionText: Which of the following abbreviated call signs of aircraft XY-ABC is correct?
What, if any, is the abbreviated call sign of "FASTAIR 2345"?
What is the correct call sign of "FASTAIR 345" in the initial call to the aerodrome control tower and the approach control unit, if the aircraft has a maximum take-off weight of 136 tonnes?

Fastair 345 has been instructed to contact Stephenville ARRIVAL on frequency 118.0. What is the correct way to indicate it will follow this instruction?
On the readability scale what does "READABILITY 5" mean?
On the readability scale what does "READABILITY 1" mean?
On the readability scale what does "READABILITY 4" mean?
On the readability scale what does "READABILITY 3" mean?
Select the correct and complete phrase to initiate a readability check:
options: ground station identification, aircraft identification, the words "readability check"
aircraft identification, the words "how do you read?"
ground station identification, aircraft identification, the words "radio check", frequency in use

questions.questionText: On the readability scale what does "READABILITY 2" mean?
Which of the following has to be read-back? 1) Taxi clearances. 2) Surface winds... 3) Climb to... 4) Turn right... 5) Runway XX in use. 6) Braking action poor.
Which of the following messages sent by ATC to an aircraft in flight must be read back? 1) Descend to... 2) Wind... 3) Turn right... 4) Reduce speed... 5) Squawk... 6) Braking action poor.
What is the correct way for the pilot to acknowledge that ATIS Information Golf has been received?
Must a general call be acknowledged?
options: Yes, from all stations in a random sequence.
Yes, from all stations in the sequence they have been addressed.
Yes, but only from the station first called.

questions.questionText: Which elements of instructions or information shall always be read back?
options: QNH, SSR code, approach aid serviceability.
SSR code, QNH, take-off clearance, speed instructions.
ATC clearance, speed instructions, runway state information.

questions.questionText: RADAR instructs aircraft X-BC: "X-BC SQUAWK STANDBY". What does this mean?
options: X-BC is requested to standby for radar vectors.
X-BC is requested to standby as the radar controller is busy.
X-BC is requested to switch to standby position.
X-BC is requested to standby on the frequency.

questions.questionText: RADAR instructs aircraft X-BC: "X-BC RESET SQUAWK 1015". What does this mean?
options: X-BC is requested to set new code 1015.
X-BC is requested to reselect SSR code 1015.
X-BC has been identified by SSR code 1015.

questions.questionText: RADAR informs aircraft X-BC: "X-BC IDENTIFIED". What does this mean?
options: X-BC should perform an identification turn.
X-BC is not visible on the radar screen.

questions.questionText: What does the phrase "SQUAWK 1234" mean?
options: Make a test transmission on 123.4 MHz.
Select code 1 234 on the SSR transponder.
Give a short count for DF (direction finder).

questions.questionText: What does the instruction "FASTAIR 345, RESET SQUAWK 1240" mean?
options: RADAR informs Fastair 345 that radar service is terminated at time 1 240.
RADAR instructs Fastair 345 to stop squawk 1 240 immediately.
Fastair 345 is requested to reselect assigned mode and code 1240.
Fastair 345 is requested to set new code 1 240.

questions.questionText: RADAR instructs aircraft X-BC: "X-BC SQUAWK IDENT". What does this mean?
options: Radar identification has been achieved by correlating an observed radar blip with aircraft XY-ABC.
X-BC shall reselect his assigned mode and code.
X-BC shall operate the IDENT button.
X-BC should perform an identification turn of at least 020 degrees.

questions.questionText: Which Q-code is used to report altitude?
If you are requested to report your height. To which Q-code setting would you refer?
A message preceded by the phrase "TRANSMITTING BLIND DUE RECEIVER FAILURE" shall be transmitted:
options: on the frequency presently in use.

questions.questionText: After suffering a radio receiver failure the pilots should make blind transmissions preceded by the phrase "TRANSMITTING BLIND DUE TO RECEIVER FAILURE" and subsequently:
options: enter the next enroute holding pattern encountered.
advise the time of the next intended transmission.

questions.questionText: In case of a SSR transponder failure occurring after departure of an IFR flight, the pilot shall:
options: land at the nearest suitable aerodrome for repair.
inform the competent ATC unit immediately.

questions.questionText: An aircraft encountering radio communication failure on an IFR flight in VMC is assumed to:
options: squawk IDENT and proceed to the alternate aerodrome.
continue the flight to destination aerodrome in any case.
continue to fly in VMC, land at the nearest suitable aerodrome, report its arrival.
leave controlled airspace and continue the flight within uncontrolled airspace.

questions.questionText: An aircraft encountering radio communication failure on an IFR flight in IMC has to land, if possible, within:
options: 30 minutes after ETA or the last EAT, whichever is later.
15 minutes after vacating the transition layer.
30 minutes after noticing the radio failure.
30 minutes after waiting for the EAT.

questions.questionText: An aircraft on an IFR flight in VMC experiences radio communication failure. The aircraft is assumed to:
options: land at the nearest suitable aerodrome.
return to the aerodrome of departure.

questions.questionText: A departing aircraft experiencing radio communication failure on an IFR flight under radar vectors has to:
options: squawk 7 600 and maintain the heading last assigned by ATC for a period of 3 minutes and then return to the flight path in accordance with the current flight plan.
squawk 7 600 and thereafter return to the route indicated in the current flight plan in the most direct manner.
squawk 7 600, maintain present heading for 1 minute and thereafter return to the route indicated in the current flight plan on the shortest way.
squawk 7 600 and thereafter, regardless of any limitation instructed by ATC, return to the route indicated in the current flight plan on the shortest way.

questions.questionText: Under which of the following circumstances shall an aircraft station squawk 7600?
options: In case of radio communication failure.

questions.questionText: If all attempts to establish radio contact with a ground station fail, the pilot of an aeroplane shall transmit messages preceded by the phrase:
options: PAN PAN, PAN PAN, PAN PAN.
READ YOU ONE, READ YOU ONE.

questions.questionText: An aircraft is squawking 7600. This indicates:
options: It is diverting to the alternate aerodrome.
It is about to make a forced landing.
It is unable to establish communication due to radio equipment failure.
It is requesting immediate level change.

questions.questionText: In case the transponder fails before the departure for an IFR flight, the pilot shall:
options: obtain prior permission by ATC to conduct the flight.
insert under item 18 of the flight plan TRANSPONDER UNSERVICABLE and inform ATC after departure.
inform FIS for relay to AIS.

questions.questionText: The expression "TRANSMITTING BLIND DUE TO RECEIVER FAILURE" implies that no answer is expected. It shall be used by:
options: an aircraft station being aware of receiver failure.
a ground station broadcasting information to all listening stations.
an aircraft station doing blind transmissions at a "non-towered" airfield.
a radar controller performing a PAR or SRE final approach.

questions.questionText: What action is required by the pilot of an aircraft station if he/she is unable to establish radio contact with an aeronautical station?
options: Land at the nearest aerodrome appropriate to the route of flight.
Try to establish communication with other aircraft or aeronautical stations.

questions.questionText: An aircraft experiencing radio communication failure on an IFR flight in IMC is assumed to:
options: execute a VMC approach at the nearest suitable aerodrome.
proceed to an area from where the flight can be continued according to the visual flight rules.
proceed in accordance with the current flight plan to the designated navigation aid serving the destination aerodrome.
divert to the most suitable aerodrome according to the route of flight.

questions.questionText: When transmitting a message preceded by the phrase "TRANSMITTING BLIND DUE TO RECEIVER FAILURE" during an enroute flight, the aircraft station shall also:
options: return to the airport of departure.
join base leg when approaching the airfield for landing.
advise the time of its next intended transmission.

questions.questionText: An aircraft station fails to establish radio contact with an aeronautical station on the designated frequency. What action is required by the pilot?
options: Continue the flight to the destination airport without any communication.
Land at the nearest airport without an ATC unit.
Attempt to establish contact with the station on an alternative frequency.
Return to the airport of departure.

questions.questionText: If an aircraft experiences a radio failure, the transponder should be set to:
Within the European Region, an aircraft experiencing radio communication failure on an IFR departure has to squawk 7600 and:
options: land at the departure aerodrome in any case.
maintain the altitude last assigned by ATC for a period of 5 minutes and then continue in accordance with the flight plan.
maintain the level last assigned by the ATC for a period of 7 minutes and then climb in accordance with the flight plan.
climb immediately to the cruising level indicated in the flight plan.

questions.questionText: When shall the pilot of an aircraft experiencing communications failure keep a watch for instructions passed by visual signals?
options: When the aircraft is entering the traffic pattern of an uncontrolled airport.
When the aircraft is forming part of the aerodrome traffic at a controlled aerodrome.
When entering a FIR during an IFR flight.

questions.questionText: An aircraft encountering radio communication failure on an IFR flight in IMC has to hold over the designated navigation aid serving the destination aerodrome:
options: 3 minutes, if an expected approach time is not acknowledged.
until the expected approach time last received and acknowledged.

questions.questionText: What do the spoken words "PAN PAN MEDICAL" mean?
options: The aircraft has an urgent need of medical care upon landing at destination airport.
The phrase/signal is inadmissible in radiotelephony.
The message which follows concerns a protected medical transport operated by aircraft assigned exclusively to medical transportation.
The aircraft has a sick passenger on board and requests priority to land.

questions.questionText: A message concerning a protected medical transport operated by aircraft assigned exclusively to medical transportation shall be preceded by the signal:
List the content of a PAN MEDICAL message in correct sequence: 1) number and type... 2) next reporting point... 3) registration... 4) "MEDICAL" 5) position 6) heading... 7) other information.

options: a condition concerning the safety of a person on board or within sight and requiring immediate assistance.
a condition concerning the attitude of an aircraft when intercepting the localizer during an ILS approach.
a condition concerning the safety of an aircraft or of a person on board, but which does not require immediate assistance.
a condition of being threatened by serious and / or imminent danger and requiring immediate assistance.

questions.questionText: A signal sent by radiotelephony consisting of the spoken words "MAYDAY MAYDAY MAYDAY" means:
options: the aircraft has a very urgent message to transmit concerning the safety of a ship, aircraft or vehicle.
the aircraft is forced to perform a fuel dumping procedure.
the aircraft has a message to transmit concerning adverse weather conditions along its route of flight.
imminent danger threatens the aircraft and immediate assistance is required.

questions.questionText: Which of the following messages shall a station in control of distress use to impose silence?
options: ALL STATIONS IN THIS FREQUENCY, MAYDAY TRAFFIC.

questions.questionText: When an aircraft is no longer in distress, it shall transmit a message cancelling the distress condition. Which words shall this message include?

When an aircraft station receives the call "ALL STATIONS STEPHENVILLE RADAR, STOP TRANSMITTING MAYDAY" it is requested:
options: to continue normal communication on the frequency in use.
to leave the frequency in use.
not to interfere with the distress communication.
to assist Stephenville RADAR in handling the distress traffic.

questions.questionText: The distress communication and silence conditions shall be terminated by transmitting a message. Which words shall this message include?

options: the air-ground frequency in use at the time.
the emergency frequency in any case.
the FIS frequency designated for the airspace concerned.

questions.questionText: An aircraft squawking 7700 indicates to the ground station that:
options: there is a sick passenger on board.

questions.questionText: Which of the following frequencies is an international emergency frequency?
An aircraft in state of emergency shall squawk:
The distress message shall contain as many as possible of the following elements/details:
options: aircraft call sign, nature of distress, pilots intention, present position, level and heading.
aircraft call sign, aerodrome of departure, position and level.
aircraft call sign, present position, assistance required.
aircraft call sign, route of flight, destination airport.

questions.questionText: When an aircraft station receives the call "ALL STATIONS STEPHENVILLE RADAR, DISTRESS TRAFFIC ENDED" it is requested:
options: to acknowledge receipt of this message.
to resume normal communication with Stephenville RADAR.
to impose silence to other stations in its vicinity.
to discontinue communication with Stephenville RADAR.

questions.questionText: Radio silence can be imposed by an aeronautical station in case of:
An aircraft in distress shall send the following signal by radiotelephony:
options: PAN PAN, PAN PAN, PAN PAN

questions.questionText: An urgency message shall be preceded by the radiotelephony urgency signal:
The urgency message to be sent by an aircraft reporting an urgency condition shall contain at least the following elements/details:
options: name of the station addressed, present position, assistance required.
aircraft call sign, destination airport, ETA at destination, route of flight.
aircraft call sign, nature of the urgency condition, pilots intention, present position, level and heading.
aircraft identification, aerodrome of departure, level and heading.

questions.questionText: A signal sent by radiotelephony consisting of the spoken words "PAN PAN, PAN PAN, PAN PAN" means:
options: the aircraft is diverting from the route cleared because of a thunderstorm and asks for immediate reclearance.
imminent danger threatens the aircraft and immediate assistance is required.
an aircraft on final approach is starting the missed approach procedure.
the aircraft has a very urgent message to transmit concerning the safety of a ship, aircraft or other vehicle, but immediate assistance is not required.

questions.questionText: On hearing an urgency message a pilot shall:
options: monitor the frequency to ensure assistance if required.
impose radio silence on the frequency in use.
change the frequency, because radio silence will be imposed on the frequency in use.

questions.questionText: Which frequency shall be used for the first transmission of an urgency call?
options: The air-ground frequency in use at the time.

questions.questionText: During approach you ask ATC about current RVR. You expect the answer to be:

What does "FRICTION COEFFICIENT 20" in a runway report mean?

options: Runway covered with wet snow and slush.

questions.questionText: When the term "OVERCAST" is used in an aviation routine weather report (METAR), the amount of clouds covering the sky is:
options: No clouds but poor ground visibility.

questions.questionText: How is the visibility in an aviation routine weather report (METAR) expressed in plain language?
options: up to 1 500 m in metres, above in kilometres.
up to 5000 m in metres, above in kilometres.

questions.questionText: What is normally used for ATIS broadcasts?

report it as soon as possible, by reporting it as a special air-report.

questions.questionText: While flying in nimbostratus clouds, the pilot sees a flash of lightning. What communication, if any, should the pilot make to ATS?

routine information to arriving and departing aircraft by means of continuous and repetitive broadcast.
information concerning enroute weather phenomena which may effect the safety of aircraft operation.
weather reports relating a specific number of aerodromes located within a flight information region (FIR).

questions.questionText: What should a pilot report during an IFR flight (weather conditions / phenomena)?
options: A lightning strike when it was forecasted.

questions.questionText: Which is the frequency separation between consecutive frequencies in the VHF band?

higher temperature and higher air density

options: Level of aircraft and terrain elevations.

questions.questionText: To which frequency bands do the frequencies 118.000 - 136.975 MHz belong?

options: allows the aircraft to be contacted on VHF and HF frequencies that are not being monitored by the flight crew.

allows two way VHF data transmission between ATC and aircraft.

questions.questionText: When a TCAS Resolution Advisory (RA) requires a deviation from an ATC clearance, pilots should report to the ATC controller as soon as practicable after he starts to deviate, with his callsign plus the phraseology:

Prior to departure an aeroplane is loaded with 16 500 litres of fuel at a fuel density of 780 kg/m³. This is entered into the load sheet as 16 500 kg and calculations are carried out accordingly. As a result of this error, the aeroplane is:
options: lighter than anticipated and the calculated safety speeds will be too low.
lighter than anticipated and the calculated safety speeds will be too high.
heavier than anticipated and the calculated safety speeds will be too low.
heavier than anticipated and the calculated safety speeds will be too high.

questions.questionText: Fuel loaded onto an aeroplane is 15 400 kg but is erroneously entered into the load and trim sheet as 14 500 kg. This error...

v, will be reached sooner than expected.
the aeroplane will rotate much earlier than expected.

questions.questionText: A pilot has finished the pre-planning. Shortly before departure an additional container with a mass of 3 500 kg of traffic load is accepted. Take-off and climb is accomplished without any problems. How will the performance of the aircraft be affected?

questions.questionText: At maximum certificated Take-off Mass, an aeroplane departs from an airfield which is not limiting for either take-off or Landing Masses. During initial climb the smaller one engine suffers a contained disintegration. An emergency is declared and the aeroplane returns to departure airfield for an immediate landing. The most likely result of this action will be:
options: a high threshold speed and possible undercarriage or other structural failure.
a high threshold speed and a shorter stop distance.
a landing short resultant from the increased angle of approach due to the very high aeroplane mass.
a landing further along the runway than normal.

questions.questionText: What is the primary purpose of mass and balance calculations?
options: To ensure the aircraft is safe to fly and operates within structural limits
To calculate the fuel cost for the trip
To determine the maximum speed of the aircraft
To select the best cruise altitude

questions.questionText: During a violent avoidance manoeuvre, a light twin aircraft certified to EASA CS-23 requirements, was subjected to an instantaneous load factor of 4.2. The Flight Manual specifies that the aircraft is certified in the normal category for a load factor of -1.9 to +3.8. Considering the certification requirements and taking into account that the manufacture of the load did not include, during its conception, a supplementary margin in the flight envelope, it might be possible to observe:
options: no distortion, permanent or temporary of the structure.
an elastic deformation whilst the load was applied, but no permanent distortion.
rupture of one or more structural components.
a permanent deformation of the structure.

questions.questionText: If an aeroplane is at a higher mass than anticipated, for a given airspeed the angle of attack will:
options: remain constant, drag will increase and endurance will increase.
be decreased, drag will decrease and endurance will increase.
remain constant, drag will decrease and endurance will decrease.
be greater, drag will increase and endurance will decrease.

questions.questionText: The term 'Dry Operating Mass' (DOM) includes:
options: Basic Empty Mass plus crew, catering, and other operating items
Basic Empty Mass plus usable fuel
Take-off Mass minus landing gear mass
The mass of the aircraft with maximum fuel

questions.questionText: Overloading has the following effects on performance:
options: reduced take-off and landing distance, increased V_NE and increased fuel consumption.
reduced take-off and landing distance, increased V_NE and reduced rate of climb.
increased take-off and landing distance, reduced rate of climb and increased fuel consumption.
increased take-off and landing distance, increased rate of climb and increased fuel consumption.

questions.questionText: When considering the effects of increased mass on an aeroplane, which of the following is true?
options: Gradient of climb for a given power setting will be higher.

questions.questionText: The 'Zero Fuel Mass' (ZFM) is defined as:
options: The mass of the aircraft including traffic load but excluding usable fuel
The mass of the aircraft with empty tanks
The mass of the aircraft including only crew and fuel
The mass of the aircraft at the end of the flight

questions.questionText: An additional baggage container is loaded into the aft cargo compartment but is not entered into the load and trim sheet. The aeroplane will be heavier than expected and calculated take-off:
options: are unaffected but V1 will be increased.

questions.questionText: In order to provide an adequate buffet boundary at the commencement of the cruise a speed of 1.3 V is used. At a mass of 120000 kg this is a CAS of 180 kts. If the mass of the service is increased to 191 kts, drag will decrease and air distance per kg of fuel will increase.
options: unaffected as Vs always occurs at the same angle of attack.
increased to 191 kts, drag will decrease and air distance per kg of fuel will increase.
increased to 191 kts, drag will increase and air distance per kg of fuel will decrease.
increased to 202 kts but, since the same angle of attack is used, drag and range will remain the same.

questions.questionText: In cruise flight, an aft centre of gravity location will:
options: not change the static curve of stability into longitudinal.
does not influence longitudinal static stability.

questions.questionText: Maximum Take-off Mass (MTOM) is a structural limit that:
options: May not be exceeded at the start of the take-off run
Is only relevant for large jet aircraft
Can be exceeded if the runway is long enough
Is always equal to the Maximum Landing Mass

questions.questionText: What determines the longitudinal stability of an aeroplane?
options: The relationship of thrust and lift to weight and drag.
The effectiveness of the horizontal stabilizer, rudder and rudder trim tab.
The location of the centre of gravity with respect to the neutral point.
The dihedral, angle of sweepback and the keel effect.

questions.questionText: An aeroplane is said to be neutrally stable. This is likely to:
options: be totally unrelated to the position of the centre of gravity.
be caused by a centre of gravity, which is towards the rearward limit.
be caused by a centre of gravity, which is towards the forward limit.
cause the centre of gravity to move forwards.

questions.questionText: Maximum Zero Fuel Mass (MZFM) is primarily limited by:
options: The bending moment at the wing root
The tire pressure of the main wheels

questions.questionText: Which of the following statements is correct?
options: The lowest stalling speed is obtained if the actual centre of gravity is located in the middle between the aft and forward limit of centre of gravity.
If the actual centre of gravity is close to the forward limit of the centre of gravity the aeroplane may be unstable, making it necessary to increase elevator forces.
A tail heavy aeroplane is less stable and stalls at a lower speed than a nose heavy aeroplane.
If the actual centre of gravity is located behind the aft limit of centre of gravity it is possible that the aeroplane will be unstable, making it necessary to increase elevator forces.

questions.questionText: When the centre of gravity is at the forward limit, an aeroplane will be:
options: extremely unstable and require excessive elevator control to change pitch.
extremely unstable and require small elevator control to change pitch.
extremely stable and require small elevator control to change pitch.
extremely stable and will require excessive elevator control to change pitch.

questions.questionText: The distance between the datum and the centre of gravity is called the:
Explain the effect of a centre of gravity (CG) which is in front of the forward limit:
options: The range decreases and the absolute ceiling increases.
The range increases and the absolute ceiling decreases.
Both the range and the absolute ceiling decrease.
Both the range and the absolute ceiling increase.

questions.questionText: The mass displacement caused by landing gear extension:
options: creates a longitudinal moment in the direction (pitch-up or pitch-down) determined by the type of landing gear.
does not create a longitudinal moment.

questions.questionText: A moment is calculated by multiplying:
For a given configuration, the stall speed of an aeroplane will be highest when loaded to a:
options: maximum allowable mass with the most aft CG.
maximum allowable mass with the most forward CG.
low gross mass with the most forward CG.
low gross mass with the most aft CG.

questions.questionText: If nose wheel moves aft during gear retraction, how will this movement affect the location of the centre of gravity (CG) on the aircraft?
options: It will cause the CG to move aft.
It will not affect the CG location.
It will cause the CG to move forward.
The CG location will change, but the direction cannot be told from the information given.

questions.questionText: The effect of the centre of gravity (CG) being close to the forward limit is:
options: a reduced rate of climb capability.
a reduction in the specific fuel consumption.
a better rate of climb capability.
a decrease in the induced drag.

questions.questionText: The datum is a fixed reference plane from which all:
An aeroplane is said to be neutrally stable. This is likely to:
options: be unrelated to the positions of the centre of pressure and the centre of gravity.
be caused by a centre of gravity, which is located at the centre of pressure.
be caused by a centre of gravity, which is located at the datum.
be caused by a centre of gravity, which is located towards the forward limit.

questions.questionText: Which of the following statements is correct?
options: The station (STA) is always the location of the centre of gravity in relation to a reference point, normally the leading edge of the wing.
The centre of gravity is given in percent of MAC calculated from the leading edge of the wing, where MAC always = the wing chord halfway between the centre line of the fuselage and the wing tip.
A tail heavy aeroplane is less stable and stalls at a lower speed than a nose heavy aeroplane.
If the actual centre of gravity is located behind the aft limit the aeroplane longitudinal stability increases.

questions.questionText: If the centre of gravity is too far forward, the aircraft will:
options: Be more stable but require more elevator force for pitch control
Be unstable and prone to stalling
Have a higher cruise speed due to reduced drag
Require less fuel for the same distance

questions.questionText: An aeroplane is loaded with its centre of gravity towards the rear limit.
options: a reduction in power required for a given speed.
an increased risk of stalling due to a decrease in tailplane moment.
an increased fuel consumption as a result of increased drag.

questions.questionText: If the centre of gravity is near the forward limit, the aeroplane will:
options: require elevator trim, which will result in an increase in fuel consumption.
require less power for a given airspeed.
benefit from reduced drag due to the decrease in angle of attack.
tend to over rotate during take-off.

questions.questionText: If the centre of gravity is too far aft, the aircraft will:
options: Become less stable and more difficult to recover from a stall
Become extremely stable and easy to fly
Have a lower stall speed due to increased lift
Require more engine thrust to maintain level flight

questions.questionText: Which of the following is most likely to affect the range of centre of gravity positions on an aeroplane?
options: The need to minimize drag forces and so improve efficiency.
The need to maintain elevator and tailplane (horizontal stabilizer) effectiveness in all flight conditions.

questions.questionText: Assuming gross mass, altitude and airspeed remain unchanged, movement of the centre of gravity from the forward to the aft limit will be reduced.
options: (incomplete options, likely "reduced maximum cruise speed" is correct)
It depends on the individual aircraft's basic empty mass.
The value is listed in the aircraft's Type Certificate Data Sheet.

questions.questionText: The Operating Mass (OM) equals:
options: empty mass plus the take-off fuel mass.
empty mass plus the trip fuel mass.
The value is listed in the aircraft's Type Certificate Data Sheet.

questions.questionText: The Mean Aerodynamic Chord (MAC) is:
options: The average chord length of the wing
The distance from the nose to the tail
The length of the main spar
The width of the horizontal stabilizer

questions.questionText: Conversion of fuel volume to mass:
options: must be done by using actual measured fuel density values.
must be done using fuel density values of 0.79 for JP 1 and 0.76 for JP 4 as specified in EASA AIR OPS.
must be done by using standard fuel density values as specified in EASA AIR OPS.
may be done by using standard fuel density values as specified by the Operations Manual, if the actual fuel density is not known.

questions.questionText: What mass has to be entered in the loading chart for aviation fuel if 170 l may be refuelled? (Fuel density = 0.78 kg/l)
CG position expressed as % MAC is calculated as:
options: (CG distance from datum - LE of MAC distance from datum) / MAC length * 100
Distance from datum to CG / MAC length * 100

questions.questionText: Basic Empty Mass (BEM) is:
options: Dry Operating Mass minus Traffic Load.
Dry Operating Mass minus fuel load.
a component of Dry Operating Mass.
the actual Take-off Mass, minus Traffic Load.

questions.questionText: The maximum quantity of fuel that can be loaded into an aircraft's tanks is given as 400 US gallons. If the fuel density (specific gravity) is given as 0.79 the mass of fuel which may be loaded is:
Traffic Load is defined as the mass of:
options: Passengers, baggage, cargo, and any non-revenue load

questions.questionText: By adding to the Basic Empty Mass (BEM) the following fixed necessary equipment for a specific flight (catering, safety and rescue equipment, fly away kit, crew), we get:

The Basic Empty Mass (BEM) of an aircraft is 30 000 kg. The masses of the following items are: Catering: 300 kg, Safety and rescue material: nil, Fly away kit: nil, Crew (inclusive crew baggage): 365 kg, Fuel at take-off: 3000 kg, Unusable fuel: 120 kg, Passengers, baggage, cargo: 8000 kg. The Dry Operating Mass (DOM) is:
options: In accordance with the EASA AIR OPS standard masses.
Check the most recent weighing schedule.

questions.questionText: Useful Load is the difference between:
options: Take-off Mass and Dry Operating Mass
Maximum Landing Mass and Basic Empty Mass
Zero Fuel Mass and Basic Empty Mass
Take-off Mass and Zero Fuel Mass

questions.questionText: Which statement regarding the relationship between traffic load and range is correct?
options: The maximum traffic load is not limited by the reserve fuel quantity.
The maximum zero fuel mass limits the maximum quantity of fuel.
The Maximum Landing Mass is basically equal to the Maximum Zero Fuel Mass.
The traffic load can be limited by the desired range.

questions.questionText: With respect to aeroplane loading in the planning phase, which of the following statements is always correct?
options: MTOM = ZFM + maximum possible fuel mass
It depends on the individual aircraft's basic empty mass.
As determined by the pilot-in-command during pre-flight planning.

questions.questionText: Determine the Zero Fuel Mass for the following single engine aeroplane. Given: Basic Empty Mass: 1764 lbs, Optional Equipment: 35 lbs, Pilot + Front seat passenger: 300 lbs, Cargo Mass: 350 lbs, Ramp Fuel (Block Fuel): 60 Gal, Trip Fuel: 35 Gal, Fuel density: 6 lbs/Gal
options: In accordance with the EASA AIR OPS standard masses.
Consult the Loading Manual for specific structural limits.

questions.questionText: Standard mass for an adult male (including hand baggage) is typically:

The Maximum Zero Fuel Mass (MZFM): 1) is a regulatory limitation. 2) is calculated for a maximum load factor of +3.5 g. 3) is based on the maximum permissible bending moment at the wing root. 4) is defined under the assumption that fuel is consumed from the outer wing tanks first. 5) is defined under the assumption that fuel is consumed from the inner wing tanks first.
options: 1 and 3 (is a regulatory limitation; is based on the maximum permissible bending moment at the wing root)
In accordance with the EASA AIR OPS standard masses.
Check the most recent weighing schedule.
It depends on the individual aircraft's basic empty mass.

questions.questionText: The Maximum Certificated Taxi (or Ramp) Mass is that mass to which an aeroplane may be loaded prior to engine start. It is:
options: a fixed value which is listed in the Flight Manual.
a value which varies with airfield temperature and altitude. Corrections are listed in the Flight Manual.
a value which is only affected by the outside air temperature. Corrections are calculated from data given in the Flight Manual.
a value which varies only with airfield altitude. Standard corrections are listed in the Flight Manual.

questions.questionText: Standard mass for an adult female (including hand baggage) is typically:
Considering only structural limitations, on long distance flights (at the aeroplane maximum range), the Traffic Load is normally limited by:
options: the Maximum Zero Fuel Mass plus the Take-off Mass.

questions.questionText: The Maximum Zero Fuel Mass (MZFM) is:
options: a structural limit listed in the Flight Manual as a fixed value.
governed by the Traffic Load to be carried.
tabulated in the Flight Manual against arguments of airfield elevation and temperature.

questions.questionText: Standard mass for a child is typically:
Which of the following statements is correct?
options: The Maximum Take-off Mass is equal to the maximum mass when leaving the ramp.
The Basic Empty Mass is equal to the mass of the aeroplane excluding Traffic Load and usable fuel but including the crew.
The Maximum Zero Fuel Mass ensures that the centre of gravity remains within limits after the uplift of fuel.
The Maximum Landing Mass of an aeroplane is restricted by structural limitations, performance limitations and the strength of the runway.

questions.questionText: The Maximum Certificated Take-off Mass is:
options: a structural limit which may not be exceeded for any take-off.
a take-off limiting mass which is affected by the aerodrome altitude and temperature.
limited by the runway take-off distance available. It is tabulated in the Flight Manual.
a take-off limiting mass which is governed by the gradient of climb after reaching V2.

questions.questionText: The density of standard aviation kerosene (Jet A-1) is approximately:
The Maximum Taxi (Ramp) Mass is governed by:
options: bearing strength of the taxiway pavement.

questions.questionText: The Maximum Structural Take-off Mass is:
options: a limit which may not be exceeded for any take-off.
limited by the take-off distance available. It is tabulated in the flight manual.
a take-off limiting mass which is affected by the aerodrome altitude and temperature.
a take-off limiting mass which is governed by the gradient of climb after reaching VY.

questions.questionText: Considering only structural limitations, on very short legs with minimum take-off fuel, the Traffic Load is normally limited by:
The density of aviation gasoline (Avgas 100LL) is approximately:
For a particular aeroplane, the structural maximum mass without any fuel on board, other than unusable quantities, is:
options: a fixed value which will limit the amount of fuel carried.
a variable value which is governed by the payload carried.
a fixed value which is stated in the Aeroplane Operating Manual.
a variable value which may limit the payload carried.

questions.questionText: The Maximum Zero Fuel Mass (MZFM) is a mass limitation for the:
options: allowable load exerted upon the wing considering a margin for fuel tanking.
total load of the fuel imposed upon the wing.

questions.questionText: An 'Index' is a simplified version of a:

Given: Maximum Ramp Mass: 69900 kg, Maximum Take-off Mass: 69300 kg, Maximum Landing Mass: 58900 kg, Maximum Zero Fuel Mass: 52740 kg, Performance limited Take-off Mass: 67450 kg, Performance limited Landing Mass: 55470 kg, Dry Operating Mass: 34900 kg, Trip Fuel: 6200 kg, Taxi Fuel: 250 kg, Contingency and final reserve fuel: 1300 kg, Alternate Fuel: 1100 kg. Calculate the maximum traffic load.

An aeroplane weighing schedule indicates that the Empty Mass is 57 320 kg. The nominal Dry Operating Mass (DOM) is 60120 kg and the Maximum Zero Fuel Mass (MZFM) is given as 72100 kg. Which of the following is a correct statement in relation to this aeroplane?
options: Operational items have a mass of 2 800 kg and the maximum Useful Load is 11 980 kg.
Operational items have a mass of 2 800 kg and the maximum Useful Load is 14 780 kg.
Operational items have a mass of 2 800 kg and the maximum Traffic Load for this aeroplane is 14 780 kg.
Operational items have a mass of 2 800 kg and the maximum Traffic Load for this aeroplane is 11 980 kg.

questions.questionText: Floor loading limits are expressed in:
options: kg per square metre or lb per square foot

questions.questionText: The medium range twin jet transport is scheduled to operate from a departure airfield where conditions limit the Take-off Mass to 65 050 kg. The destination airfield has a performance limited Landing Mass of 54 500 kg. The Dry Operating Mass is 34 900 kg. Loading data: Taxi Fuel: 350 kg, Trip Fuel: 9250 kg, Contingency and final reserve fuel: 1100 kg, Alternate Fuel: 1000 kg, Traffic Load: 18600 kg. Which statement correctly describes the situation?
options: The aircraft may be safely operated with the stated Traffic and Fuel Load.
The aircraft is Zero Fuel Mass limited and the Traffic Load must be reduced to 14 170 kg.
The aircraft is Landing Mass limited and the Traffic Load must be reduced to 17 500 kg.
The aircraft may be safely operated with an additional 200 kg of Traffic Load.

questions.questionText: If the Dry Operating Mass is 40970 kg the maximum Traffic Load (TL) that can be carried on this flight is:

Given: Basic Empty Mass: 1764 lbs, Optional Equipment: 35 lbs, Pilot + Passenger: 300 lbs, Cargo: 350 lbs, Ramp Fuel (Block Fuel): 60 Gal, Trip Fuel: 35 Gal, Taxi Fuel: 1.7 Gal, Final Reserve Fuel: 18 Gal, Fuel density: 6 lbs/Gal. What is the expected landing mass (LM)?
options: The value is listed in the aircraft's Type Certificate Data Sheet.
In accordance with the EASA AIR OPS standard masses.

questions.questionText: Linear (running) load limits are expressed in:

Given: Dry Operating mass: 85000 kg, Performance Limited Take-off Mass: 127000 kg, Performance Limited Landing Mass: 98500 kg, Maximum Zero Fuel Mass: 89800 kg, Trip Fuel: 29300 kg, Contingency and final reserve fuel: 3600 kg, Alternate Fuel: 2800 kg. The maximum Traffic Load (TL) that can be carried on this flight is:
Standard mass as used in the computation of passenger load establishes the mass of a child as:
options: 35 kg irrespective of age provided they occupy a seat.
35 kg for children over 2 years occupying a seat and 10 kg for infants (less than 2 years) occupying a seat.
35 kg only if they are over 2 years old and occupy a seat.
35 kg for children over 2 years occupying a seat and 10 kg for infants (less than 2 years) not occupying a seat.

questions.questionText: What happens to the CG as fuel is consumed from wing tanks located aft of the datum?

Given: Maximum Structural Landing Mass: 125000 kg, Maximum Zero Fuel Mass: 108500 kg, Maximum Structural Take-off Mass: 155000 kg, Dry Operating Mass: 82000 kg, Scheduled Trip Fuel: 17000 kg, Reserve Fuel: 5000 kg. Assuming performance limitations are not restricting, the maximum permitted Take-off Mass (TOM) and maximum Traffic Load (TL) are respectively:

The Dry Operating Mass (DOM) of an aircraft is 2 000 kg. The Maximum Take-off Mass (MTOM), Landing (MLM) and Zero Fuel Mass (MZFM) are identical at 3 500 kg. The Block Fuel Mass is 550 kg, and the Taxi Fuel Mass is 50 kg. The available mass of payload is:
The Basic Empty Mass (BEM) of an aircraft is determined by:
options: Adding up the weights of all parts from the manual
Calculating it from the fuel flow
Guessing based on the aircraft size

questions.questionText: Given: Dry Operating Mass: 38000 kg, Maximum Structural Take-off Mass: 72000 kg, Maximum Landing Mass: 65000 kg, Maximum Zero Fuel Mass: 61000 kg, Fuel burn: 8000 kg, Take-off Fuel: 10300 kg. The maximum allowed Take-off Mass (TOM) and Traffic Load (TL) are respectively:

An aeroplane is to depart from an airfield at a Take-off Mass (TOM) of 302 550 kg. Fuel on board at take-off (including contingency and alternate of 19 450 kg) is 121 450 kg. The Dry Operating Mass (DOM) is 161 450 kg. The Useful Load (UL) will be:
How often should an aircraft be re-weighed if fleet masses are used?

The following data is extracted from an aeroplane loading manifest: Take-off Mass (Performance Limited): 93 500 kg, Maximum Certificated Landing Mass: 86 300 kg, Take-off Fuel on board: 16 500 kg. During the flight a diversion is made to an en-route alternate which is not performance limited for landing. Fuel remaining at landing is 10 300 kg. The Landing Mass:
options: must be reduced to 81 700 kg in order to avoid a high speed approach.
is 87 300 kg and excess structural stress could result.
Check the most recent weighing schedule.
As determined by the pilot-in-command during pre-flight planning.

questions.questionText: Given: Maximum Ramp Mass: 69900 kg, Maximum Take-off Mass: 69300 kg, Maximum Landing Mass: 58900 kg, Maximum Zero Fuel Mass: 52740 kg, Dry Operating Mass: 34900 kg, Trip Fuel: 11800 kg, Taxi Fuel: 500 kg, Contingency and final reserve fuel: 7600 kg, Alternate Fuel: 7900 kg. Take-off and Landing Mass are not performance limited. The maximum Traffic Load (TL) that can be carried is:
options: In accordance with the EASA AIR OPS standard masses.
The value is listed in the aircraft's Type Certificate Data Sheet.

questions.questionText: Based on actual conditions, an aeroplane has the following performance Take-off Mass limitations: Flaps: 0°: 4100 kg, 10°: 4400 kg, 15°: 4600 kg; Climb: 0°: 4700 kg, 10°: 4500 kg, 15°: 4200 kg. Structural limitations: Take-off / Landing / Zero Fuel: 4300 kg. The Maximum Take-off Mass is:
During a weighing procedure, the aircraft must be:
options: Clean, dry, and in an enclosed hangar
Outside on the ramp with engines running
Fully loaded with fuel and passengers
In any condition as long as the scales are calibrated

questions.questionText: The following data applies to an aeroplane which is about to take-off: Certified Maximum Take-off Mass: 141 500 kg, Performance Limited Take-off Mass: 137 300 kg, Dry Operating Mass: 58 400 kg, Crew and crew hand baggage mass: 640 kg, Crew baggage in hold: 110 kg, Fuel on board: 60 700 kg. From this data calculate the mass of the Useful Load (UL).
options: It depends on the individual aircraft's basic empty mass.
In accordance with the EASA AIR OPS standard masses.

questions.questionText: Dry Operating Mass: 34 900 kg, Performance Limited Take-off Mass: 66 300 kg, Performance Limited Landing Mass: 55 200 kg, Maximum Zero Fuel Mass: 53 070 kg. Fuel requirements: Taxi Fuel: 400 kg, Trip Fuel: 8 600 kg, Contingency Fuel: 430 kg, Alternate Fuel: 970 kg, Holding Fuel: 900 kg, Traffic Load: 16 600 kg. The total fuel which may be safely loaded at the parking gate prior to departure is:
The 'Lever Principle' states that equilibrium is achieved when:
options: The sum of the clockwise moments equals the sum of the anti-clockwise moments
The masses on both sides are equal
The force is applied at the center

questions.questionText: The operator of an aircraft equipped with 50 seats uses standard masses for passengers and baggage. During the preparation of a scheduled flight a group of passengers present themselves at the check-in desk, it is apparent that even the lightest of these exceeds the value of the declared standard mass.
options: The operator should determine the actual mass of such passengers by weighing or by adding an adequate mass increment.
The operator is obliged to use the actual masses of each passenger.
The operator may use the standard masses for the load and balance calculation without correction.
The operator may use the standard masses for the balance but must correct these for the load calculation.

questions.questionText: In determining the Dry Operating Mass of an aeroplane it is common practice to use standard mass values for crew. These values are:
options: flight crew (male) 88 kg (female) 75 kg, cabin crew 75 kg each. These do not include an allowance for hand baggage.
flight crew (male) 88 kg (female) 75 kg, cabin crew 75 kg each. These include an allowance for hand baggage.
flight crew 85 kg, cabin crew 75 kg each. These do not include a hand baggage allowance.
flight crew 85 kg, cabin crew 75 kg each. These are inclusive of a hand baggage allowance.

questions.questionText: What is the effect of an aft CG on fuel consumption?
options: It slightly decreases fuel consumption due to reduced tailplane downforce
It increases fuel consumption due to increased drag
It has no effect on fuel consumption
It makes the engines run hotter

questions.questionText: The centre of gravity of a body is that point:
options: where the sum of the moments from the external forces acting on the body is equal to zero.
which is always used as datum when computing moments.
through which the sum of the forces of all masses of the body is considered to act.
where the sum of the external forces is equal to zero.

questions.questionText: The centre of gravity of an aircraft is that point through which the Total Mass of the aircraft is said to act. The weight acts in a direction:
options: governed by the distribution of the mass within the aeroplane.
at right angles to the flight path.
always parallel to the aeroplane's vertical axis.

questions.questionText: A 'Load Manifest' or 'Load Sheet' must be signed by:
The centre of gravity of an aircraft:
options: must be maintained in a fixed position by careful distribution of the load.
may only be moved if permitted by the regulating authority and endorsed in the aircraft's certificate of airworthiness.
can be allowed to move between defined limits.
is in a fixed position and is unaffected by aircraft loading.

questions.questionText: The weight of an aircraft, which is in level non accelerated flight, is said to act:
options: always along the vertical axis of the aeroplane.
vertically through the centre of pressure.
vertically through the centre of gravity.

questions.questionText: A load placed aft of the datum:
options: has a positive arm and therefore generates a positive moment.
has a negative arm and therefore generates a negative moment but a positive mass.
has a positive arm and therefore generates a positive moment but negative mass.
has a negative arm and therefore generates a negative moment and mass.

questions.questionText: A Last Minute Change (LMC) is allowed if it is:
options: Within specified limits defined in the Operations Manual

questions.questionText: A location in the aircraft which is identified by a number designating its distance from the datum is known as:
options: As determined by the pilot-in-command during pre-flight planning.
The value is listed in the aircraft's Type Certificate Data Sheet.
Consult the Loading Manual for specific structural limits.

questions.questionText: A load placed forward of the datum:
options: has a negative arm and therefore generates a negative mass and moment.
has a positive arm and therefore generates a positive mass and moment.
has a negative arm and therefore generates a negative moment.
has a positive arm and therefore generates a positive moment.

questions.questionText: The term 'Balance Arm' is synonymous with:
The datum used for balance calculations is:
options: chosen on the longitudinal axis of the aircraft and necessarily situated between the leading edge and trailing edge of the wing.
chosen on the longitudinal axis of the aircraft, and always at the firewall level.
chosen on the longitudinal axis of the aeroplane, and necessarily situated between the nose and the tail of the aircraft.
chosen on the longitudinal axis of the aeroplane, but not necessarily between the nose and the tail of the aircraft.

questions.questionText: The distance from the datum to the Centre of Gravity of a mass is known as:
options: the Moment Arm or Balance Arm.

questions.questionText: What is 'Ballast' used for?
options: To move the CG into the allowable range
To increase the aircraft's top speed

questions.questionText: Which one of the following is correct?
With reference to mass and balance calculations (on an aeroplane) a datum point is used. This datum point is:
options: the point through which the sum of the mass values (of the aeroplane and its contents) is assumed to act vertically.
a fixed point from which all Balance Arms are measured. It may be located anywhere on the aeroplane's longitudinal axis or on the extensions to that axis.
a point from which all Balance Arms are measured. The location of this point varies with the distribution of loads on the aeroplane.
a point near the centre of the aeroplane. It moves longitudinally as masses are added forward and aft of its location.

questions.questionText: Refer to figure 031-51. Which mass at location "X" is required to bring the see-saw in a state of equilibrium?
If the CG moves behind the 'Neutral Point', the aircraft becomes:

Imagine a 5 m long lever. The lever is suspended by a pivot which is located at a distance of 3 m from one end. If a force of 600 N is pulling down on the long end of the lever, which force is necessary on the short end for obtaining equilibrium?

A mass of 600 kg is loaded at a station which is located 12 metres behind the present centre of gravity and 18 metres behind the datum. The moment for that mass used in the loading manifest is (Assume: g=10 m/s²):
The Maximum Landing Mass (MLM) is usually lower than the MTOM because:
options: The landing gear structure is limited by the impact forces of landing
The runway is shorter than the takeoff run
The aircraft is too heavy to stop

questions.questionText: Refer to figure. Given Force Fa: 300 N, Force Fb: 150 N, Distance A: 3 m. Calculate distance B to obtain equilibrium:
For the following see-saw to be in balance:
options: Check the most recent weighing schedule.
In accordance with the EASA AIR OPS standard masses.
As determined by the pilot-in-command during pre-flight planning.

questions.questionText: Which item is NOT included in the Dry Operating Mass?

Separate azimuth and elevation transmitters, DME facility.

Decrease in area and move to the top of the screen.

Primary radar in the SHF band.

questions.questionText: When and from which station is an SPI (Special Position Identification) pulse sent?

With two or more aircraft in different directions, same altitude, slant range > 1.7 NM.
When two or more aircraft are in approximately the same direction from the ground station with a difference in slant range of more than 1.7 NM.

can occur at any altitude and only in the horizontal plane.
